turns.content: My app has about 20 components that need shared state. Is Redux overkill?

It depends on what kind of shared state you have and how often it changes. For a medium-sized app with ~20 components sharing state, Redux can be either “solid infrastructure” or unnecessary ceremony. A good way to decide is to classify your state into a few buckets: 1) Server state (data fetched from APIs): best handled by React Query / TanStack Query, SWR, Apollo, etc., not Redux. 2) UI state (modal open, selected tab, filters): usually fine with React Context or a small store like Zustand. 3) Client domain state (cart, editor draft, multi-step workflow, permissions): could fit Redux, Zustand, or Context depending on complexity and debugging needs. Redux is most compelling when you need: - predictable updates and strict patterns - time-travel debugging / action logs - middleware pipelines (logging, analytics, persistence, side effects) - large teams needing consistent conventions If your shared state is mostly simple UI state and a bit of client domain state, Zustand or Context is often simpler. If you find yourself passing callbacks and state through many layers, Context helps, but you need to watch re-render patterns. What kinds of state are those 20 components sharing? More UI flags, or domain entities, or async data?

It’s a mix. Some global UI like theme, sidebar open, notifications. Some domain state like a shopping cart and a “builder” workflow where many components update a shared draft. Also a few auth bits like user roles. Data fetching is handled by React Query already.

Given that, you can keep React Query for server state and pick a client-state tool for the rest. The cart + builder workflow is where the choice matters most, because multiple components update the same draft and you’ll want predictable updates and good dev tooling. A pragmatic breakdown: - Theme: Context is perfect (rare changes). - Sidebar open: local state or Context (small). - Notifications/toasts: often a small global store (Zustand is great). - Auth roles: Context is fine; or a store if you need derived permissions and frequent checks. - Cart: store (Zustand or Redux) depending on complexity. - Builder draft: store (Zustand or Redux) because many components will read/write and you may need undo/redo, validation, persistence, etc. If you want minimal boilerplate and good performance, Zustand is a strong fit. If you anticipate complex flows, want strict patterns, and value Redux DevTools action trace with a formal reducer model, Redux Toolkit (RTK) is the modern “not overkill” version. Key question: Do you need: - undo/redo for the builder? - complex computed selectors (e.g., pricing rules, constraints)? - logging/auditing of changes? - persistence across refresh? - SSR/hydration constraints?

Undo/redo would be nice for the builder but not required right now. Pricing rules might get complex: discounts, bundles, and some validations. Persistence across refresh for the cart is required. Builder draft persistence would be nice. SSR is not in scope.

That points to a store with: - derived state / selectors - persistence middleware - easy incremental adoption Both Redux Toolkit and Zustand can do this well. Redux Toolkit: - Strong for complex domain logic: reducers, slices, memoized selectors via Reselect, standardized patterns. - Great DevTools story: action log, time travel, inspect state diffs. - Persistence: redux-persist works, or custom middleware. - Slightly more ceremony: slices, actions, provider, hooks. Zustand: - Very low boilerplate: create store + hooks. - Persistence middleware built-in (persist). - Derived state can be done via selectors or computed getters, but you need discipline to avoid scattered logic. - DevTools integration exists, but not as standardized across teams. If pricing/validation logic is going to grow and you want to keep it centralized and testable, Redux Toolkit is attractive. If you want speed and simplicity and are okay enforcing conventions yourself, Zustand is attractive. A hybrid is common: Zustand for UI + cart, Context for theme/auth, and keep builder logic in a dedicated store module. But if builder becomes the core of the app, Redux Toolkit might be the “future-proof” choice. How many engineers will touch this state logic, and do you care about enforcing a consistent pattern?
Assume multiple engineers, but not a huge team. I do care about consistency and avoiding “everyone does it differently.” But I also don’t want a ton of boilerplate.

Then Redux Toolkit is worth serious consideration, because it gives a consistent, documented pattern without forcing you to write classic Redux boilerplate. RTK reduces boilerplate via: - createSlice (auto-generated action creators + reducer) - Immer under the hood (mutating syntax) - createAsyncThunk (if you ever need it, though you’re using React Query) - configureStore (sensible defaults) - RTK Query (you’re not using it, but it’s there) You can still keep it lean by scoping Redux only to client domain state (cart, builder, notifications) and leaving theme/auth in Context if you prefer. Or put those in Redux too for consistency. Zustand can be consistent if you define conventions: store per domain, immutable updates or Immer, selectors, action naming, and code review rules. But the framework won’t enforce it as strongly. If you want “consistent patterns with moderate boilerplate,” Redux Toolkit is the safe, widely recognized option. If you want “minimal code with conventions enforced socially,” Zustand is excellent. One way to decide: prototype one slice (cart) in both and compare: - lines of code - ease of testing - persistence setup - readability for new contributors Want me to sketch what a cart store looks like in RTK vs Zustand, including persistence?
Yes. Show both, including persistence and an example selector for total price with discounts.

Redux Toolkit example (cart slice + persistence concept): 1) State shape idea: - items: { [productId]: { id, name, price, qty } } - discounts: array of rules or applied codes - derived: computed via selector (not stored) Cart slice: // cartSlice.ts import { createSlice, PayloadAction, createSelector } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' type CartItem = { id: string; name: string; price: number; qty: number } type Discount = { code: string; type: 'percent' | 'fixed'; value: number } type CartState = { items: Record<string, CartItem> discounts: Discount[] } const initialState: CartState = { items: {}, discounts: [] } const cartSlice = createSlice({ name: 'cart', initialState, reducers: { addItem(state, action: PayloadAction<CartItem>) { const item = action.payload const existing = state.items[item.id] if (existing) existing.qty += item.qty else state.items[item.id] = item }, setQty(state, action: PayloadAction<{ id: string; qty: number }>) { const { id, qty } = action.payload if (qty <= 0) delete state.items[id] else if (state.items[id]) state.items[id].qty = qty }, removeItem(state, action: PayloadAction<string>) { delete state.items[action.payload] }, applyDiscount(state, action: PayloadAction<Discount>) { state.discounts.push(action.payload) }, removeDiscount(state, action: PayloadAction<string>) { state.discounts = state.discounts.filter(d => d.code !== action.payload) }, clearCart(state) { state.items = {} state.discounts = [] }, }, }) export const cartActions = cartSlice.actions export default cartSlice.reducer Selectors (with discount logic): // selectors.ts import { createSelector } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' import type { RootState } from './store' const selectCart = (state: RootState) => state.cart export const selectCartItems = createSelector( selectCart, cart => Object.values(cart.items) ) export const selectSubtotal = createSelector( selectCartItems, items => items.reduce((sum, it) => sum + it.price * it.qty, 0) ) export const selectTotal = createSelector( [selectSubtotal, selectCart], (subtotal, cart) => { let total = subtotal for (const d of cart.discounts) { if (d.type === 'percent') total -= total * (d.value / 100) else total -= d.value } return Math.max(0, Math.round(total * 100) / 100) } ) Persistence: - Option A: redux-persist (common) - Option B: custom subscribe + localStorage Simple custom persistence: // store.ts import { configureStore } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' import cartReducer from './cartSlice' function loadCartState() { try { const raw = localStorage.getItem('cart') if (!raw) return undefined return { cart: JSON.parse(raw) } } catch { return undefined } } export const store = configureStore({ reducer: { cart: cartReducer }, preloadedState: loadCartState(), }) store.subscribe(() => { const state = store.getState() localStorage.setItem('cart', JSON.stringify(state.cart)) }) export type RootState = ReturnType<typeof store.getState> export type AppDispatch = typeof store.dispatch Usage: // in component const total = useSelector(selectTotal) const dispatch = useDispatch<AppDispatch>() dispatch(cartActions.addItem({ id, name, price, qty: 1 })) Zustand example (store + persist + selector): import { create } from 'zustand' import { persist } from 'zustand/middleware' type CartItem = { id: string; name: string; price: number; qty: number } type Discount = { code: string; type: 'percent' | 'fixed'; value: number } type CartState = { items: Record<string, CartItem> discounts: Discount[] addItem: (item: CartItem) => void setQty: (id: string, qty: number) => void removeItem: (id: string) => void applyDiscount: (d: Discount) => void removeDiscount: (code: string) => void clearCart: () => void subtotal: () => number total: () => number } export const useCartStore = create<CartState>()( persist( (set, get) => ({ items: {}, discounts: [], addItem: (item) => set((state) => { const existing = state.items[item.id] const qty = (existing?.qty ?? 0) + item.qty return { items: { ...state.items, [item.id]: { ...item, qty }, }, } }), setQty: (id, qty) => set((state) => { const next = { ...state.items } if (qty <= 0) delete next[id] else if (next[id]) next[id] = { ...next[id], qty } return { items: next } }), removeItem: (id) => set((state) => { const next = { ...state.items } delete next[id] return { items: next } }), applyDiscount: (d) => set((state) => ({ discounts: [...state.discounts, d] })), removeDiscount: (code) => set((state) => ({ discounts: state.discounts.filter((d) => d.code !== code) })), clearCart: () => set({ items: {}, discounts: [] }), subtotal: () => { const items = Object.values(get().items) return items.reduce((sum, it) => sum + it.price * it.qty, 0) }, total: () => { let total = get().subtotal() for (const d of get().discounts) { total = d.type === 'percent' ? total - total * (d.value / 100) : total - d.value } return Math.max(0, Math.round(total * 100) / 100) }, }), { name: 'cart' } ) ) Usage: const total = useCartStore((s) => s.total()) const addItem = useCartStore((s) => s.addItem) Tradeoffs: - Redux selectors are memoized and centralized; Zustand’s computed functions are easy but can be recomputed unless you manage it carefully. - Zustand persist is trivial. - Redux persistence is a bit more setup but also more explicit and testable. If you want a Zustand version with memoized selectors (so total doesn’t recompute unnecessarily), or an RTK version with redux-persist and a whitelist, I can show that too.
How do re-renders differ between Context, Redux, and Zustand? I’m worried about performance when many components subscribe.

React Context: any component that uses a context value will re-render when the provider value changes by reference, even if the component only uses part of it (unless you split contexts or use context selectors via external libs). So a “big context object” is a common performance footgun. Redux (react-redux): components subscribe to the store, and useSelector runs on every dispatch, but components only re-render if the selected value changes by strict equality. This is efficient if selectors are small and stable. Memoized selectors help avoid recomputation and keep referential stability. Zustand: components subscribe to slices via selectors too. A component only re-renders if the selected slice changes by strict equality (or via custom equality function). Similar to react-redux in that sense, but with less ceremony. Practical implications: - If you put {cart, builder, ui, auth} into one Context value, any update to any part forces all consumers to re-render. - If you split into multiple contexts (CartContext, BuilderContext, UIContext), you can reduce blast radius. - Redux and Zustand naturally encourage slice subscriptions. For your case (cart + builder with frequent updates): - Context can work if you carefully split and avoid passing unstable objects/functions. - Redux/Zustand are usually safer by default for performance. Also consider update frequency: builder draft updates might happen on every keystroke. You’ll want fine-grained subscriptions: e.g., components subscribe to only the field they care about. Do you expect builder updates to be high-frequency (typing, dragging sliders), and do you need to keep the entire draft in sync live?
Yes, builder updates can be frequent. There are controls like sliders and text inputs. Many panels show derived values from the draft.

Then I’d avoid a single Context storing the whole draft. You can still use Context if you implement a reducer + split contexts + memoization, but it’s easy to get wrong. Better options: - Zustand with selector-based subscriptions per field/derived slice. - Redux with normalized state and selectors, plus possibly batching updates. For high-frequency input: 1) Keep local component state while typing, commit on blur/debounce for global store, if real-time global sync isn’t required. 2) If real-time global sync is required, ensure only the affected subscribers re-render. Zustand pattern: - store draft as nested object - components select specific path: useStore(s => s.draft.someField) - use shallow equality for selecting object subsets - derived computations can be done in selectors or separate derived store; be careful not to recompute huge things per keystroke. Redux pattern: - update draft field via action - components select field via useSelector - derived values via memoized selectors - heavy derived calculations can be memoized per relevant inputs If you have panels showing derived values, memoization matters. Redux’s selector ecosystem is very mature. Zustand can do it too, but you have to structure it. Do you anticipate derived computations being heavy (e.g., pricing engine), or mostly simple arithmetic?
Pricing could get heavy, with many rules. I’d like it testable and deterministic. But I don’t want to lock into Redux if Zustand can do the same.

You can make pricing deterministic and testable regardless of state library by isolating it into pure functions that take the draft/cart and return computed outputs. Then the store just holds inputs; selectors call the pure pricing engine. Example approach: - pricingEngine.ts exports computePrice(input): output - store holds draft/cart + applied discounts - selector: selectPricingOutput = createSelector(inputs..., computePrice) in Redux, or in Zustand a selector hook that calls computePrice on selected inputs Redux makes memoization and recomputation control more straightforward: - createSelector caches based on input references - you can structure inputs so they change only when necessary - you can unit test selectors or the pure engine Zustand can also do: - useCartStore(s => computePrice(s.items, s.discounts)) but that recomputes on any relevant state change; you can mitigate by selecting only needed primitives and using shallow equality, but memoization is less standardized. - Or compute and store derived outputs in state and update them in actions (but then you risk bugs where derived state gets out of sync). A robust, library-agnostic approach: - Keep pricing computation outside. - Use memoization library (reselect, memoize-one) even with Zustand. If you want minimal lock-in, you can: - Write pure reducers/actions in a domain layer. - Use Zustand/Redux as adapters. But practically, adopting Redux Toolkit doesn’t lock you out much; slices are plain reducers and actions. The bigger lock-in is react-redux hooks and store setup, which is manageable. Are you already using TypeScript? That affects ergonomics too.
Yes, TypeScript. Also, I want to avoid prop drilling and avoid excessive boilerplate. How would you structure state modules if using Redux Toolkit?

A clean RTK structure for your case: src/ app/ store.ts hooks.ts (typed useDispatch/useSelector) features/ cart/ cartSlice.ts cartSelectors.ts cartTypes.ts builder/ builderSlice.ts builderSelectors.ts builderTypes.ts pricingEngine.ts ui/ uiSlice.ts (sidebar, notifications) uiSelectors.ts auth/ authSlice.ts (roles, user) authSelectors.ts Key principles: - “Feature folder” per domain. - Slice contains state + reducers + actions. - Selectors live next to slice; keep them reusable. - Derived heavy logic in pure modules (pricingEngine.ts). - Keep React Query separate; don’t mirror server state in Redux. Typed hooks: // app/hooks.ts import { TypedUseSelectorHook, useDispatch, useSelector } from 'react-redux' import type { RootState, AppDispatch } from './store' export const useAppDispatch = () => useDispatch<AppDispatch>() export const useAppSelector: TypedUseSelectorHook<RootState> = useSelector Store: // app/store.ts import { configureStore } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' import cartReducer from '../features/cart/cartSlice' import builderReducer from '../features/builder/builderSlice' import uiReducer from '../features/ui/uiSlice' import authReducer from '../features/auth/authSlice' export const store = configureStore({ reducer: { cart: cartReducer, builder: builderReducer, ui: uiReducer, auth: authReducer, }, }) export type RootState = ReturnType<typeof store.getState> export type AppDispatch = typeof store.dispatch Persistence: - persist only cart + maybe builder draft, not everything. - easiest is redux-persist with whitelist, or custom subscribe per slice. If you want to reduce boilerplate further: - group simple UI state in one uiSlice - keep only domain-heavy stuff in separate slices - avoid createAsyncThunk since React Query handles async This structure gives consistent conventions across engineers. If you want, I can show a builderSlice pattern for field updates that supports high-frequency updates without huge action boilerplate (e.g., setField with path), and how to keep it type-safe.
Show a builderSlice with a generic setField approach, but keep it reasonably type-safe. Also show how derived pricing selectors would work.

For “generic setField” you’re trading some type safety for convenience. In TS, truly type-safe deep path updates are possible but can get complex. A pragmatic compromise: Option A (recommended): explicit actions for major fields, generic for “advanced”: - setName, setQuantity, setOption... - plus setDraftValue(path, value) for rarely used dynamic updates Option B: use a typed key for top-level fields only (keeps it simple). Here’s Option B: top-level keys typed, nested handled explicitly. Types: type BuilderDraft = { productId: string | null quantity: number options: { size: 'S' | 'M' | 'L' color: string } extras: Array<{ id: string; enabled: boolean }> discountCode?: string } Slice: import { createSlice, PayloadAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' type BuilderState = { draft: BuilderDraft } const initialState: BuilderState = { draft: { productId: null, quantity: 1, options: { size: 'M', color: 'black' }, extras: [], }, } type TopLevelDraftKey = keyof BuilderDraft const builderSlice = createSlice({ name: 'builder', initialState, reducers: { setDraftField<K extends TopLevelDraftKey>( state, action: PayloadAction<{ key: K; value: BuilderDraft[K] }> ) { const { key, value } = action.payload state.draft[key] = value }, setOption( state, action: PayloadAction<{ key: keyof BuilderDraft['options']; value: string }> ) { const { key, value } = action.payload // narrow if you want stricter typing per key ;(state.draft.options as any)[key] = value }, toggleExtra(state, action: PayloadAction<{ id: string }>) { const ex = state.draft.extras.find(e => e.id === action.payload.id) if (ex) ex.enabled = !ex.enabled else state.draft.extras.push({ id: action.payload.id, enabled: true }) }, resetDraft(state) { state.draft = initialState.draft }, }, }) export const builderActions = builderSlice.actions export default builderSlice.reducer Usage: dispatch(builderActions.setDraftField({ key: 'quantity', value: 2 })) dispatch(builderActions.setOption({ key: 'color', value: 'red' })) Derived pricing: - pricingEngine.ts: pure function type PricingInput = { draft: BuilderDraft; cartItems: Array<{id:string; price:number; qty:number}>; discounts: any[] } type PricingOutput = { total: number; breakdown: Array<{ label: string; amount: number }> } export function computePricing(input: PricingInput): PricingOutput { // heavy logic here, deterministic & testable return { total: 123, breakdown: [] } } Selectors with memoization: import { createSelector } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' import type { RootState } from '../../app/store' import { computePricing } from './pricingEngine' import { selectCartItems } from '../cart/cartSelectors' const selectBuilderDraft = (s: RootState) => s.builder.draft const selectCartDiscounts = (s: RootState) => s.cart.discounts export const selectPricing = createSelector( [selectBuilderDraft, selectCartItems, selectCartDiscounts], (draft, cartItems, discounts) => computePricing({ draft, cartItems, discounts }) ) export const selectPricingTotal = createSelector( [selectPricing], pricing => pricing.total ) Why this helps: - computePricing only runs when draft/cartItems/discounts references change. - If you keep draft updates granular, only dependent selectors recompute. Caveat: If draft is a big object and you replace it wholesale often, references change and pricing recomputes. With Immer updates in slice, only changed paths update, but the top object reference will still change; however, createSelector caching is based on input references. Since draft reference changes on any field update, pricing recomputes every time anyway. That might be acceptable, but if pricing is heavy, you might want to: - split draft inputs: select only relevant fields for pricing - or memoize inside computePricing with internal caching - or debounce pricing updates - or compute pricing in a web worker If pricing depends on only some fields, you can create a selector that picks those fields into a smaller object to reduce recomputation triggers, but any change to those fields still recomputes. What’s the rough size of the draft and how heavy could pricing get (e.g., milliseconds per compute)?
Could be tens of milliseconds if rules grow. I’d like to keep typing responsive. Could we avoid recalculating pricing on every keystroke?

Yes. Common strategies: 1) Debounce pricing computation in UI - Keep draft updates immediate, but compute pricing output in a debounced selector-like hook. - Downside: derived values lag slightly. 2) Commit-on-idle / commit-on-blur - Keep input local while typing, dispatch to global store after debounce or on blur. - This is often the best UX for text inputs. - For sliders, you can update on drag with throttle, and commit final value on release. 3) Split draft into “stable” and “transient” - Store transient typing state locally, store stable values globally. - Pricing uses stable values. 4) Move pricing off the main thread - Web worker: computePricing in worker, postMessage results. - Keep UI responsive even if compute is heavy. If you want to keep global draft in sync while typing but avoid heavy pricing recompute, you can decouple pricing from store updates: - store raw inputs in Redux/Zustand - pricing computed in a separate service with debounce and caching - store latest pricing output in store, updated asynchronously (still deterministic if you treat it as derived cache) In Redux, you could: - dispatch setDraftField on each input - have a listenerMiddleware that watches draft changes and triggers debounced pricing computation, then dispatches pricingUpdated(result) - components read pricing from store, which updates at a controlled rate This keeps typing responsive because pricing work is moved out of render and can be debounced. Zustand can do similar with subscribe() and debounce. If you like, I can show an RTK listenerMiddleware setup for debounced pricing updates, and a Zustand subscribe-based equivalent.
Show both approaches. I’m especially interested in RTK listenerMiddleware.

RTK listenerMiddleware approach: 1) Add pricing slice/cache: - builder slice holds draft - pricing slice holds last computed pricing output + status pricingSlice.ts: import { createSlice, PayloadAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' type PricingOutput = { total: number; breakdown: Array<{ label: string; amount: number }> } type PricingState = { value: PricingOutput | null updatedAt: number | null } const initialState: PricingState = { value: null, updatedAt: null } const pricingSlice = createSlice({ name: 'pricing', initialState, reducers: { pricingUpdated(state, action: PayloadAction<PricingOutput>) { state.value = action.payload state.updatedAt = Date.now() }, pricingCleared(state) { state.value = null state.updatedAt = null }, }, }) export const pricingActions = pricingSlice.actions export default pricingSlice.reducer 2) Listener middleware: - watch builderActions.setDraftField / setOption / toggleExtra, etc. - debounce computePricing, then dispatch pricingUpdated store.ts: import { configureStore, createListenerMiddleware, isAnyOf } from '@reduxjs/toolkit' import builderReducer, { builderActions } from '../features/builder/builderSlice' import cartReducer from '../features/cart/cartSlice' import pricingReducer, { pricingActions } from '../features/builder/pricingSlice' import { computePricing } from '../features/builder/pricingEngine' const listener = createListenerMiddleware() listener.startListening({ matcher: isAnyOf( builderActions.setDraftField, builderActions.setOption, builderActions.toggleExtra // include cart actions too if pricing depends on cart ), effect: async (action, api) => { // Cancel previous pending runs for debounce behavior api.cancelActiveListeners() // Wait for user to pause changes await api.delay(150) const state = api.getState() as RootState const draft = state.builder.draft const cartItems = Object.values(state.cart.items) const discounts = state.cart.discounts // Potentially heavy compute const pricing = computePricing({ draft, cartItems, discounts }) api.dispatch(pricingActions.pricingUpdated(pricing)) }, }) export const store = configureStore({ reducer: { builder: builderReducer, cart: cartReducer, pricing: pricingReducer, }, middleware: (getDefault) => getDefault().prepend(listener.middleware), }) Now components: - input components update draft immediately - derived pricing panels read from state.pricing.value - pricing updates at most every 150ms after last change You can tune delay, add conditions, or offload to worker. Zustand subscribe + debounce: import { create } from 'zustand' import { subscribeWithSelector } from 'zustand/middleware' import debounce from 'lodash.debounce' import { computePricing } from './pricingEngine' type State = { draft: BuilderDraft cartItems: CartItem[] discounts: Discount[] pricing: PricingOutput | null setDraftField: <K extends keyof BuilderDraft>(key: K, value: BuilderDraft[K]) => void // ... } export const useStore = create<State>()( subscribeWithSelector((set, get) => ({ draft: initialDraft, cartItems: [], discounts: [], pricing: null, setDraftField: (key, value) => set((s) => ({ draft: { ...s.draft, [key]: value } })), })) ) const recompute = debounce(() => { const { draft, cartItems, discounts } = useStore.getState() const pricing = computePricing({ draft, cartItems, discounts }) useStore.setState({ pricing }) }, 150) useStore.subscribe( (s) => [s.draft, s.cartItems, s.discounts], () => recompute(), { equalityFn: (a, b) => a === b } // note: array ref changes; you might refine this ) Better: subscribe to specific slices and call recompute when they change. RTK listenerMiddleware is cleaner because debouncing and cancellation are built-in and you keep logic near store configuration. If pricing computation is tens of ms, you might still want a web worker, but debouncing often solves “typing lag”. Do you want pricing to update while dragging sliders (throttled) but not on every pixel, or is delayed update okay?
Throttled updates for sliders, debounced for text inputs. Is that feasible with Redux? How would you implement it cleanly?

Yes. The cleanest approach is to treat input components differently: - Text input: local state updates on each keystroke; dispatch to store on debounce or on blur. - Slider: dispatch on throttle during drag; dispatch final value on pointer up. This keeps store updates (and listener-triggered pricing recompute) at a manageable rate. Implementation sketch: TextInput component: - local value state - useEffect debounce to dispatch builderActions.setDraftField - onBlur dispatch immediately Slider component: - onChange (drag) throttled dispatch - onChangeEnd dispatch final With Redux, you can implement throttle/debounce in the component layer (simplest) and keep store logic straightforward. Example text input: const DraftNameInput = () => { const dispatch = useAppDispatch() const name = useAppSelector(s => s.builder.draft.name) const [local, setLocal] = useState(name) useEffect(() => setLocal(name), [name]) // if external updates occur useEffect(() => { const id = setTimeout(() => { if (local !== name) dispatch(builderActions.setDraftField({ key: 'name', value: local })) }, 250) return () => clearTimeout(id) }, [local, name, dispatch]) return <input value={local} onChange={e => setLocal(e.target.value)} onBlur={() => dispatch(...)} /> } Slider: - use a throttle helper (lodash.throttle) memoized const DraftQtySlider = () => { const dispatch = useAppDispatch() const qty = useAppSelector(s => s.builder.draft.quantity) const throttled = useMemo( () => throttle((v: number) => dispatch(builderActions.setDraftField({ key: 'quantity', value: v })), 50), [dispatch] ) return ( <Slider value={qty} onChange={(v) => throttled(v)} onChangeEnd={(v) => dispatch(builderActions.setDraftField({ key: 'quantity', value: v }))} /> ) } Then listenerMiddleware can still debounce pricing to avoid recompute storms. Alternative: put the throttling/debouncing in listenerMiddleware by tagging actions with metadata, but that gets complicated. Component-level control is usually clearest. If you want to standardize this pattern, build reusable hooks: - useDebouncedDispatch(actionCreator, delay) - useThrottledDispatch(actionCreator, interval) Would you prefer a pattern where components stay dumb and all scheduling happens in middleware, or is component-level scheduling acceptable?
Component-level scheduling is acceptable. But I’m still considering Zustand. Are there pitfalls with Zustand in larger apps?

Zustand scales well, but common pitfalls in larger apps are mostly about discipline and architecture: 1) Unstructured growth - People add state/actions ad hoc; store becomes a “god store.” Mitigation: split stores per domain, or use slices pattern. 2) Derived state scattered - If everyone computes derived values inline in components, logic duplicates and becomes inconsistent. Mitigation: centralize derived logic in selector functions or pure modules. 3) Memoization and performance - It’s easy to accidentally select big objects and cause re-renders. Mitigation: always select the smallest slice; use shallow equality when selecting objects; avoid returning new objects from selectors. 4) Testing conventions - Zustand actions are easy to test, but you need to establish patterns for resetting store between tests. 5) DevTools consistency - Zustand devtools exist, but action naming and logging conventions require discipline. 6) TypeScript ergonomics for complex updates - Deep updates can get verbose or require Immer; you decide the pattern. Redux Toolkit solves some of this with “one obvious way” patterns and a mature ecosystem. A reasonable heuristic: - If you expect lots of business logic, complex derivations, and want strong conventions across engineers: RTK. - If you want simplicity and are okay enforcing conventions via code review: Zustand. If you’re leaning Zustand, I’d recommend: - use immer middleware for deep updates - use persist for cart - use subscribeWithSelector for derived caches (like debounced pricing output) - define a folder structure similar to Redux features to keep things organized Want an example of a “slices” pattern in Zustand to keep it modular?
Yes, show a modular Zustand structure with slices: cart, builder, ui, auth. Include persist only for cart. Also show how to reset state for tests.

You can build a single store composed of slices, or multiple stores. Single store with slices is common so you can access cross-slice state easily. Structure: src/state/ store.ts slices/ cartSlice.ts builderSlice.ts uiSlice.ts authSlice.ts selectors/ pricingSelectors.ts (or pricingEngine.ts) Types: // state/store.ts import { create } from 'zustand' import { devtools } from 'zustand/middleware' import { subscribeWithSelector } from 'zustand/middleware' import { createCartSlice, CartSlice } from './slices/cartSlice' import { createBuilderSlice, BuilderSlice } from './slices/builderSlice' import { createUiSlice, UiSlice } from './slices/uiSlice' import { createAuthSlice, AuthSlice } from './slices/authSlice' export type AppState = CartSlice & BuilderSlice & UiSlice & AuthSlice export const useAppStore = create<AppState>()( devtools( subscribeWithSelector((...a) => ({ ...createCartSlice(...a), ...createBuilderSlice(...a), ...createUiSlice(...a), ...createAuthSlice(...a), })), { name: 'app-store' } ) ) Cart slice with persist only for cart: - Since we’re composing slices into one store, persisting only part requires partialize. cartSlice.ts: import { StateCreator } from 'zustand' import { persist } from 'zustand/middleware' import type { AppState } from '../store' export type CartItem = { id: string; name: string; price: number; qty: number } export type Discount = { code: string; type: 'percent' | 'fixed'; value: number } export type CartSlice = { cartItems: Record<string, CartItem> cartDiscounts: Discount[] cartAddItem: (item: CartItem) => void cartSetQty: (id: string, qty: number) => void cartClear: () => void } const cartSliceImpl: StateCreator<AppState, [], [], CartSlice> = (set, get) => ({ cartItems: {}, cartDiscounts: [], cartAddItem: (item) => set((s) => { const existing = s.cartItems[item.id] const qty = (existing?.qty ?? 0) + item.qty return { cartItems: { ...s.cartItems, [item.id]: { ...item, qty } } } }, false, 'cart/addItem'), cartSetQty: (id, qty) => set((s) => { const next = { ...s.cartItems } if (qty <= 0) delete next[id] else if (next[id]) next[id] = { ...next[id], qty } return { cartItems: next } }, false, 'cart/setQty'), cartClear: () => set({ cartItems: {}, cartDiscounts: [] }, false, 'cart/clear'), }) export const createCartSlice: StateCreator<AppState, [], [], CartSlice> = persist( cartSliceImpl as any, { name: 'cart-storage', partialize: (state: AppState) => ({ cartItems: state.cartItems, cartDiscounts: state.cartDiscounts, }), // optional: versioning/migrate } ) as any Note: Zustand middleware typing gets tricky when composing; many teams accept a bit of `as any` around persist composition. Another approach is to persist a separate cart store to avoid typing friction. Builder slice: export type BuilderDraft = { /* ... */ } export type BuilderSlice = { draft: BuilderDraft setDraftField: <K extends keyof BuilderDraft>(key: K, value: BuilderDraft[K]) => void resetDraft: () => void } export const createBuilderSlice: StateCreator<AppState, [], [], BuilderSlice> = (set) => ({ draft: initialDraft, setDraftField: (key, value) => set((s) => ({ draft: { ...s.draft, [key]: value } }), false, 'builder/setDraftField'), resetDraft: () => set({ draft: initialDraft }, false, 'builder/reset'), }) UI slice: export type UiSlice = { sidebarOpen: boolean setSidebarOpen: (v: boolean) => void toasts: Array<{ id: string; message: string }> addToast: (msg: string) => void removeToast: (id: string) => void } Auth slice similarly. Reset state for tests: - Keep initial state creators and expose a reset function that sets to initial values and clears persistence. In store.ts, you can export a reset helper: const initialSnapshot = () => useAppStore.getState() But that captures mutated state. Better: define initial values in each slice module and reuse. Example: // state/reset.ts import { useAppStore } from './store' export function resetAppState() { useAppStore.setState((s) => ({ // reset each slice explicitly cartItems: {}, cartDiscounts: [], draft: initialDraft, sidebarOpen: false, toasts: [], user: null, roles: [], }), true) // clear persisted storage for cart localStorage.removeItem('cart-storage') } In tests: beforeEach(() => resetAppState()) You can also use Zustand’s “store per test” pattern by creating a store factory rather than a singleton, which is cleaner for tests: - export createAppStore() and in app use a singleton, in tests create fresh store. If you want cleaner typing and persistence isolation, I’d recommend separate stores: - useCartStore (persisted) - useBuilderStore - useUiStore - useAuthStore Then cross-store dependencies are a bit more manual, but it avoids persist composition complexity. Do you prefer a single composed store or separate stores per domain?
Separate stores per domain might be cleaner. But how do you handle cross-domain derived data, like pricing needing builder draft and cart discounts?

With separate stores, you have a few options: 1) Compute derived data in a hook that reads from both stores - useBuilderStore + useCartStore in the same component/hook - computePricing with memoization Downside: computation happens in render unless you offload/debounce. 2) Create a “derived store” (pricing store) that subscribes to both stores and maintains cached pricing output - pricing store updates when inputs change (debounced/throttled) - components read pricing store only This mirrors the Redux listenerMiddleware approach. 3) Keep a single “domain store” for things that are tightly coupled (builder + pricing), and keep cart separate. - builder store subscribes to cart store for discounts changes if needed. Option 2 is often best if you want to avoid heavy compute in render. Example derived pricing store: import { create } from 'zustand' import debounce from 'lodash.debounce' import { useBuilderStore } from './builderStore' import { useCartStore } from './cartStore' import { computePricing } from '../pricingEngine' type PricingState = { pricing: PricingOutput | null updatedAt: number | null recomputeNow: () => void } export const usePricingStore = create<PricingState>((set) => ({ pricing: null, updatedAt: null, recomputeNow: () => { const draft = useBuilderStore.getState().draft const discounts = useCartStore.getState().discounts const items = Object.values(useCartStore.getState().items) const pricing = computePricing({ draft, cartItems: items, discounts }) set({ pricing, updatedAt: Date.now() }) }, })) const debouncedRecompute = debounce(() => { usePricingStore.getState().recomputeNow() }, 150) useBuilderStore.subscribe( (s) => s.draft, () => debouncedRecompute() ) useCartStore.subscribe( (s) => [s.items, s.discounts], () => debouncedRecompute() ) Now components: const pricing = usePricingStore(s => s.pricing) This keeps compute off the render path and centralized. You’ll want to ensure subscriptions are set up once (module initialization is fine in client-only apps). In SSR, you’d need to avoid module-level subscriptions, but SSR is out of scope. This approach works similarly to Redux listener middleware: state changes trigger debounced recompute. If you adopt Redux, you get this pattern “in framework” with listenerMiddleware; with Zustand, you implement it yourself. Does having module-level subscriptions feel acceptable, or would you prefer derived computation triggered explicitly (e.g., only on “Apply” button)?
Module-level subscriptions are acceptable. Another question: how does React Context compare if I just split contexts? Could that be enough?

Split contexts can work, but for your “builder draft updated frequently + many derived panels” case, Context still has some sharp edges: - Each context update triggers re-render of all consumers of that context, regardless of what part they use. - You can split into many contexts (one per field or per subdomain), but that becomes unwieldy. - You can use useContextSelector (external library) to get selector-like subscriptions, but that’s essentially introducing a state library anyway. If you split contexts into: - ThemeContext (rare updates) -> fine - AuthContext (rare) -> fine - CartContext (moderate) -> okay if careful - BuilderContext (high-frequency) -> risky unless you adopt selector-based context For builder, you’d likely end up: - BuilderStateContext + BuilderDispatchContext (to avoid re-render from dispatch function changes) - Use reducer for updates - Memoize provider value But consumers still re-render when state changes. So yes: Context can be enough if: - builder updates are not frequent, or - you keep builder state local and only commit occasionally, or - you use context selectors library Given your requirements, Redux or Zustand is a better fit for builder/cart. If you want to keep dependencies minimal, Zustand is small and acts like “Context with selectors.” Are you trying to minimize bundle size or external dependencies?
Bundle size matters somewhat, but not extremely. I’m more concerned about maintainability and developer experience. Also, what about debugging? Redux DevTools is nice.

Debugging is a major differentiator. Redux: - Redux DevTools is the gold standard: action log, time travel, state diff, import/export, persist across reload. - With RTK, action names are consistent (slice/action). - Middlewares and listenerMiddleware integrate nicely. Zustand: - Has devtools middleware that integrates with Redux DevTools extension. - You can name actions (as shown with third argument to set), which improves logs. - Time travel works depending on how you integrate; it’s not always as seamless as Redux, but it’s decent. Context: - No built-in action log; you can add custom logging but it’s ad hoc. If your team values strong debugging and consistent patterns, Redux Toolkit is hard to beat. Zustand can get close if you adopt conventions: - Always name actions in set(..., false, 'slice/action') - Use devtools middleware in all stores - Keep actions pure-ish and centralized One more dimension: code splitting / lazy loading features. Redux supports dynamic reducer injection patterns; Zustand stores can be imported where needed too, but you need to manage initialization. Given you’re medium-sized, I’d lean: - RTK for builder + cart (core domain) - or Zustand with strong conventions if you prioritize simplicity Do you already have an established pattern in the codebase, or is this greenfield?
Greenfield. If we pick Redux Toolkit, how do we avoid overusing it for everything? I don’t want every boolean flag in Redux.

Set boundaries up front: Rule of thumb: - Local UI state stays local (component state) unless multiple distant components need it. - Global UI state that many parts need (e.g., global toast queue) can go to a uiSlice. - Domain state that’s central and shared (cart, builder) goes to Redux. - Theme/auth can be Context if updates are rare, or Redux if you want consistency. Write it down as a short architecture guideline, e.g.: 1) Server state: React Query only. 2) Form input ephemeral state: local, commit to store on submit/blur. 3) Shared domain state: Redux slices. 4) Global UI state: uiSlice only if needed by more than N areas. This prevents “everything goes in Redux” by default. Also, keep slices cohesive: - Avoid a giant “appSlice.” - Put flags next to the feature they affect. If you want to keep Redux minimal, you can have just: - cartSlice - builderSlice - uiSlice (toasts, global modal) And keep auth/theme in Context. That’s still consistent enough because the complex parts share a pattern. Want a sample “state ownership” guideline you can include in the repo docs?
Sure, but keep it brief. Also, can you compare Redux Toolkit vs Zustand in terms of testing?

Brief guideline: - Use React Query for anything fetched from APIs (lists, entities, caches). - Use component state for ephemeral UI (input typing, hover, local toggles). - Use global store for shared domain state used across routes/sections (cart, builder draft, permissions). - Use global UI slice/store for cross-cutting UI (toasts, global dialogs) only when multiple distant components need it. - Keep derived/business logic in pure functions; store holds inputs and minimal cached outputs. Testing comparison: Redux Toolkit: - Reducers are pure functions; easy to unit test by calling reducer(previousState, action). - Selectors can be unit tested with sample states. - Listener middleware can be tested with RTK utilities or integration tests; slightly more work but doable. - Strong determinism: actions -> reducers -> new state. Zustand: - Actions can be tested by creating a store instance (ideally via a factory) and calling actions, then asserting state. - If you use a singleton store, tests must reset state between runs; better to export createStore() for tests. - Subscriptions and derived stores (like pricing) can be tested but require controlling timers for debounce and ensuring subscriptions are set up. Both are testable. Redux has more “pure function” testing by default; Zustand often becomes “store integration testing,” unless you keep logic in pure modules (recommended). If you expect heavy business logic, isolate it into pure functions regardless of library; then testing is straightforward either way.
If you had to pick one for this app, which would you pick and why? Assume the builder workflow is core and will grow.

I’d pick Redux Toolkit. Reasoning: - Core builder workflow will grow: more rules, validations, derived computations, maybe undo/redo later. - Multiple engineers: RTK provides a consistent structure and “one obvious way” to add state, actions, selectors. - Debugging: Redux DevTools is excellent for complex flows; being able to inspect action sequences is valuable. - Performance: react-redux selectors and memoization patterns are well-understood; you can control recomputation and re-renders. - Extensibility: listenerMiddleware gives you a clean place for debounced pricing updates, persistence hooks, analytics, etc. You can still keep it pragmatic: - Don’t put everything in Redux—keep local state local. - Use React Query for server state. - Use RTK slices only for shared domain + global UI. Zustand is a strong alternative, but in “core workflow will grow” scenarios, teams often appreciate Redux’s structure and tooling. If you want a lightweight starting point, begin with only cart + builder slices and add ui slice later if needed.
Ok. One last thing: how would you implement undo/redo later in Redux Toolkit for builder?

Common pattern: store past/present/future for the draft (or for a subset of builder state). State shape: type UndoState<T> = { past: T[]; present: T; future: T[] } Builder state: { draftHistory: UndoState<BuilderDraft> } Reducers: - on any “mutating” action: push present to past (cap length), set present to new, clear future - undo: move last past to present, push current present to future - redo: move first future to present, push current present to past Implementation options: 1) Wrap builder reducer with an “undoable” higher-order reducer (like redux-undo style). 2) Implement directly in builderSlice, handling only actions you want tracked. Direct implementation sketch: const MAX_HISTORY = 50 type BuilderState = { history: { past: BuilderDraft[]; present: BuilderDraft; future: BuilderDraft[] } } const initial: BuilderState = { history: { past: [], present: initialDraft, future: [] } } function commit(state: BuilderState, nextPresent: BuilderDraft) { state.history.past.push(state.history.present) if (state.history.past.length > MAX_HISTORY) state.history.past.shift() state.history.present = nextPresent state.history.future = [] } reducers: { setDraftField(state, action) { const { key, value } = action.payload const next = { ...state.history.present, [key]: value } commit(state, next) }, undo(state) { const prev = state.history.past.pop() if (!prev) return state.history.future.unshift(state.history.present) state.history.present = prev }, redo(state) { const next = state.history.future.shift() if (!next) return state.history.past.push(state.history.present) state.history.present = next } } Selectors: selectDraft = s => s.builder.history.present Notes: - For high-frequency updates (typing), you may not want every keystroke in history. You can: - commit only on blur/submit, or - batch changes (e.g., commit after debounce), or - have a “checkpoint” action that pushes to history. This is another place where Redux’s action log helps: you can decide what’s undoable by which actions you record. If you want, I can show how to integrate undo checkpoints with your earlier debounce/throttle input strategy so history remains meaningful.

This JOIN query is taking ~8 seconds on about 2M rows. I’m on PostgreSQL 14. Here’s the query and EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). Can you help optimize it with indexing and maybe query rewrite? Query: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id LEFT JOIN refunds r ON r.order_id = o.id WHERE o.created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND c.is_active = true AND (r.id IS NULL OR r.status <> 'completed') GROUP BY o.id, o.created_at, c.email ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100; EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS): Limit  (cost=145234.32..145234.57 rows=100 width=72) (actual time=8110.233..8110.401 rows=100 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=12435 read=98765 dirtied=0 written=0 ->  Sort  (cost=145234.32..145884.45 rows=260050 width=72) (actual time=8110.231..8110.358 rows=100 loops=1) Sort Key: o.created_at DESC Sort Method: top-N heapsort  Memory: 54kB Buffers: shared hit=12435 read=98765 ->  HashAggregate  (cost=127645.11..132846.11 rows=260050 width=72) (actual time=7821.942..8030.992 rows=280112 loops=1) Group Key: o.id, c.email Batches: 1  Memory Usage: 101233kB Buffers: shared hit=12435 read=98765 ->  Hash Join  (cost=54789.65..116243.31 rows=2600500 width=56) (actual time=2190.222..6010.882 rows=2902210 loops=1) Hash Cond: (oi.order_id = o.id) Buffers: shared hit=12435 read=98765 ->  Seq Scan on order_items oi  (cost=0.00..51123.00 rows=3000000 width=20) (actual time=0.041..981.374 rows=3000000 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=112 read=51011 ->  Hash  (cost=51987.54..51987.54 rows=224168 width=44) (actual time=2183.188..2183.191 rows=230022 loops=1) Buckets: 262144  Batches: 1  Memory Usage: 20755kB Buffers: shared hit=12323 read=47754 ->  Hash Join  (cost=11234.12..51987.54 rows=224168 width=44) (actual time=415.624..2077.603 rows=230022 loops=1) Hash Cond: (o.customer_id = c.id) Buffers: shared hit=12323 read=47754 ->  Hash Left Join  (cost=9023.11..48111.37 rows=286213 width=28) (actual time=280.473..1787.711 rows=300118 loops=1) Hash Cond: (o.id = r.order_id) Filter: ((r.id IS NULL) OR (r.status <> 'completed'::text)) Rows Removed by Filter: 12113 Buffers: shared hit=12110 read=44211 ->  Seq Scan on orders o  (cost=0.00..35012.00 rows=500000 width=24) (actual time=0.052..944.320 rows=480202 loops=1) Filter: ((created_at >= (now() - '30 days'::interval)) AND (status = ANY ('{paid,shipped}'::text[]))) Rows Removed by Filter: 19798 Buffers: shared hit=12012 read=35000 ->  Hash  (cost=7150.00..7150.00 rows=150000 width=12) (actual time=278.845..278.847 rows=150000 loops=1) Buckets: 131072  Batches: 2  Memory Usage: 4380kB Buffers: shared hit=98 read=9211 ->  Seq Scan on refunds r  (cost=0.00..7150.00 rows=150000 width=12) (actual time=0.034..158.724 rows=150000 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=98 read=9211 ->  Hash  (cost=1400.00..1400.00 rows=80000 width=24) (actual time=134.037..134.039 rows=80000 loops=1) Buckets: 131072  Batches: 1  Memory Usage: 4856kB Buffers: shared hit=213 read=3543 ->  Seq Scan on customers c  (cost=0.00..1400.00 rows=80000 width=24) (actual time=0.022..66.874 rows=80000 loops=1) Filter: is_active Rows Removed by Filter: 20000 Buffers: shared hit=213 read=3543 Planning Time: 2.101 ms Execution Time: 8110.663 ms Schema notes: orders ~500k rows, order_items ~3M rows, refunds ~150k, customers ~100k. Current indexes: orders_pkey(id), customers_pkey(id), order_items_pkey(id), refunds_pkey(id) orders_customer_id_idx (customer_id) order_items_order_id_idx (order_id) refunds_order_id_idx (order_id) What do you suggest?

The plan is dominated by two things: 1) You’re scanning entire `order_items` (3M rows) and hashing/joining it to a fairly large set of orders (~480k after filters). That produces ~2.9M joined rows, then you aggregate them (hash aggregate uses ~100MB) and only then you sort/top-N for the LIMIT 100. 2) You’re also scanning `orders` (seq scan) and `customers` (seq scan) and `refunds` (seq scan). The fact that those are seq scans isn’t automatically bad, but with 8s runtime and lots of disk reads (`read=98765` buffers) it suggests you’re I/O bound and not using selective indexes early enough. A key issue: You have `LIMIT 100 ORDER BY o.created_at DESC`, but the plan can’t exploit that because the joins/aggregation happen before the limit is applied. We want to push down “find the top 100 recent orders that match filters” before expanding to items and aggregating. Two main approaches: A) Query rewrite with a CTE/subquery to select candidate orders first (using an index on `orders(created_at)` plus status), then join to items and aggregate. B) Indexes to support filtering and joining efficiently, and potentially use partial indexes. First, create an index that matches the WHERE + ORDER BY on orders: - You filter by `created_at >= now() - interval '30 days'` and `status IN (...)` and you order by `created_at DESC`. A good index: `(status, created_at DESC)` or `(created_at DESC)` plus status. Which is better depends on selectivity. If “paid/shipped” is most orders, then `created_at` is the big filter. If status is selective, put it first. I’d start with: CREATE INDEX orders_status_created_at_idx ON orders (status, created_at DESC); Optionally include customer_id and id to allow an index-only scan / reduce heap lookups: CREATE INDEX orders_status_created_at_cover_idx ON orders (status, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (id, customer_id); Postgres 14 supports INCLUDE. Second, customers filter `is_active = true`. Since you always join by id anyway, indexing `is_active` alone isn’t helpful; but a partial index can help if many are inactive and you often only want active: CREATE INDEX customers_active_id_idx ON customers (id) WHERE is_active; But since `customers` is only 100k rows, the seq scan isn’t huge; still, in combination it can help. Third, refunds: you have a LEFT JOIN with condition `(r.id IS NULL OR r.status <> 'completed')`. This is tricky because it prevents a simple anti-join unless you rewrite. Semantically, you want orders where there is no refund with status = 'completed'? Actually your predicate allows: - no refund row at all (r.id is null), OR - refund exists but status is not completed But if an order has multiple refunds, one completed and one pending, the LEFT JOIN will produce multiple rows and the filter will keep the non-completed row(s), effectively still including the order even though it has a completed refund. Is that intended? Many people actually mean “exclude orders that have any completed refund”. If that’s what you mean, you want NOT EXISTS. If your intent is “exclude only those orders whose refund status is completed (and only one refund per order)”, then current is OK but still inefficient. I’d propose rewriting as: WHERE NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id = o.id AND r.status = 'completed' ) This becomes an anti-join and can use an index on refunds(order_id, status) or a partial index: CREATE INDEX refunds_completed_order_id_idx ON refunds(order_id) WHERE status='completed'; or a general: CREATE INDEX refunds_order_id_status_idx ON refunds(order_id, status); Fourth, order_items: you already have `order_items(order_id)`. But the plan still seq scans order_items. That happens because it expects to join a huge set of orders (230k+), so it chooses to scan all items and hash join. If we reduce the candidate orders drastically (top 100 recent), then it can use an index nested loop into order_items and only touch items for those 100 orders. So the big win is query shape: find the 100 orders first. Rewrite: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id = o.id AND r.status='completed' ) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id WHERE c.is_active = true GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; That changes semantics slightly: you pick 100 orders before filtering by active customers. If you need “top 100 after all filters including active customers”, then pull the active condition into the CTE by joining customers there. Example: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id=o.customer_id AND c.is_active WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id = o.id AND r.status='completed' ) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN customers c ON c.id = ro.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, c.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; The second join to customers is redundant; you can select email in the CTE: WITH recent_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id=o.customer_id AND c.is_active WHERE ... ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ro.id, ro.created_at, ro.email, SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM recent_orders ro JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = ro.id GROUP BY ro.id, ro.created_at, ro.email ORDER BY ro.created_at DESC; To support this, indexes: - orders(status, created_at desc) include (id, customer_id) - refunds(order_id) WHERE status='completed' - order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price) maybe. Postgres can do index-only scan if visibility map is favorable, but even without, it can reduce heap visits: CREATE INDEX order_items_order_id_cover_idx ON order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); Try the rewrite first; it typically turns seconds into tens of milliseconds if only ~100 orders are expanded. Also run ANALYZE after creating indexes. Before proceeding: can you confirm refunds cardinality per order and intended semantics (exclude any completed refund vs something else)?

Good catch: There can be multiple refunds per order. The intended semantics are: exclude orders that have any refund with status='completed'. Pending/failed refunds shouldn’t exclude. So NOT EXISTS is correct. Also, “top 100 after all filters” is required (active customers included). I can add indexes. But I’m worried Postgres might inline the CTE and still do a big join. How do I ensure it uses the LIMIT early?

In PostgreSQL 14, CTEs are inlined by default (unless marked MATERIALIZED). Inlining is usually good, but here you specifically want the planner to preserve the “LIMIT 100 recent orders” boundary so it doesn’t expand to millions of items. You have a few options: 1) Use a subquery with ORDER BY/LIMIT that the planner can’t easily pull up past joins (often it still can, but less likely if you only select columns needed for joins). 2) Use `WITH ... AS MATERIALIZED` to force materialization. 3) Use `LATERAL` to compute totals per order after selecting 100 orders (very effective). 4) Use `DISTINCT ON` patterns or window functions; less direct. Given you need aggregation per order, the LATERAL approach is often the cleanest and guarantees “pick 100 orders, then compute total for each”. Example: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email, totals.total FROM ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.customer_id FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id AND c.is_active WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id = o.id AND r.status='completed' ) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = o.id ) totals ON true ORDER BY o.created_at DESC; Here, the subquery returns at most 100 order ids. The lateral aggregate then runs 100 small index scans into `order_items`. That’s usually fast if `order_items(order_id)` is indexed. To avoid re-joining customers twice, just select email in the subquery: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.email, totals.total FROM ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id AND c.is_active WHERE ... ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) o JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = o.id ) totals ON true ORDER BY o.created_at DESC; Now, ensuring LIMIT early: - Because the aggregation is in a LATERAL subquery correlated on `o.id`, Postgres can’t compute totals before it knows which 100 orders it’s dealing with. So it naturally does “top-N orders” first, then totals. Indexes needed: - Orders: to support the top-N selection efficiently: CREATE INDEX orders_status_created_at_idx ON orders (status, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (id, customer_id); If `status` is low-selectivity, consider (created_at DESC) INCLUDE (status, id, customer_id) and rely on filter. But since you have an IN list with two statuses, (status, created_at) often works well. - Customers: join by PK already; `is_active` filter happens during join. If most customers are active, no need. If many inactive, a partial index on (id) WHERE is_active can help, but PK lookup is already O(log n). Since you’re joining from orders to customers, it’s a lookup per order row; with top 100 orders, this is trivial anyway. The bigger benefit is when scanning lots of orders; but we’re reducing it to 100. - Refunds anti-join: CREATE INDEX refunds_completed_order_id_idx ON refunds(order_id) WHERE status='completed'; This makes NOT EXISTS extremely cheap. - Order_items: You already have order_items(order_id). Consider INCLUDE (qty, unit_price) for index-only potential: CREATE INDEX order_items_order_id_inc_idx ON order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); One more thing: your original plan scanned 480k orders even though you only need last 30 days. That implies “last 30 days” is almost the whole table, or the filter isn’t selective. But you’re ordering by created_at and limiting to 100; an index on created_at desc should make it fast regardless, because it can stop after it finds 100 qualifying rows (with some extra due to filters). So an alternative orders index: CREATE INDEX orders_created_at_desc_idx ON orders (created_at DESC) INCLUDE (id, customer_id, status); Then the planner can do an index scan backward on created_at, apply status filter, and stop at 100. Which is better depends on distribution: - If statuses are selective, (status, created_at) is best. - If created_at is the primary driver for “top recent”, (created_at desc) is best. Given you’re doing top-N by created_at, I’d lean toward: CREATE INDEX orders_created_at_desc_inc_idx ON orders (created_at DESC) INCLUDE (id, customer_id, status); Then keep your existing orders_customer_id_idx (might be redundant). Can you share approximate distribution: what fraction of orders are paid/shipped, and how many orders are in last 30 days?

Last 30 days is about 120k orders. Status distribution: paid ~40%, shipped ~20%, others ~40%. So paid+shipped ~60%. Refunds: only about 2% of orders have a completed refund. Customers: 80% active. I can create indexes, but want minimal set. Also, would BRIN help for created_at?

Minimal, high-impact set for this workload: 1) Orders index to accelerate “top recent paid/shipped”: Because you need ORDER BY created_at DESC + LIMIT and created_at is time-correlated, a btree on created_at DESC is typically enough. But you also filter on status. With 60% match, status filter isn’t super selective. The key is the index scan can walk newest orders and check status until it accumulates 100 matches. Expected to read maybe a few hundred index entries, not 120k. So: CREATE INDEX orders_created_at_desc_inc_idx ON orders (created_at DESC) INCLUDE (id, customer_id, status); This supports: - ordered retrieval - avoids heap fetch for status check in some cases (still may need heap depending on visibility map, but INCLUDE helps if index-only possible) If you see it still reading too many because of status filter, then replace with: CREATE INDEX orders_status_created_at_desc_inc_idx ON orders (status, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (id, customer_id); But that index can’t directly satisfy global ORDER BY created_at across two statuses without a sort/merge. Postgres can do two index scans (one per status) and merge, but it’s not always chosen. The plain created_at index is often simpler for top-N. 2) Refunds partial index for anti-join: CREATE INDEX refunds_completed_order_id_idx ON refunds (order_id) WHERE status='completed'; This will make the NOT EXISTS check near-constant time per candidate order. 3) Order_items index: you already have order_items(order_id). That’s sufficient for lateral aggregation. INCLUDE might help but is optional. If your order_items rows are wide or heap fetch is costly, add: CREATE INDEX order_items_order_id_inc_idx ON order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); But start without it since you already have the basic index. BRIN for created_at: - BRIN is good for large append-only tables where you scan ranges (e.g., “last 30 days”) and want to avoid scanning older blocks. But here you want top-N newest rows, and a btree on created_at is better for ORDER BY ... LIMIT because it can seek to the end and read backward. - A BRIN could help if you were doing large range scans without needing ordering, but your query is top-N. So: btree is the right tool. Recommended query using LATERAL and NOT EXISTS: SELECT o.id, o.created_at, o.email, totals.total FROM ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE c.is_active AND o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id = o.id AND r.status='completed' ) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 100 ) o JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = o.id ) totals ON true ORDER BY o.created_at DESC; This ensures only 100 order_ids trigger order_items lookups. One more nuance: if an order can have zero items (shouldn’t, but if possible), SUM returns NULL; you can COALESCE to 0. After adding indexes, run: ANALYZE orders; ANALYZE refunds; ANALYZE order_items; (or VACUUM ANALYZE if needed). If you try this, paste the new EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS).

I created: - orders_created_at_desc_inc_idx on (created_at desc) include (id, customer_id, status) - refunds_completed_order_id_idx on (order_id) where status='completed' Then ran the LATERAL query. Here’s EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS): Sort  (cost=10234.88..10235.13 rows=100 width=72) (actual time=62.041..62.066 rows=100 loops=1) Sort Key: o.created_at DESC Sort Method: quicksort  Memory: 37kB Buffers: shared hit=918 read=112 ->  Nested Loop  (cost=0.99..10231.56 rows=100 width=72) (actual time=1.883..61.630 rows=100 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=915 read=112 ->  Limit  (cost=0.56..124.56 rows=100 width=52) (actual time=1.250..3.249 rows=100 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=115 read=18 ->  Nested Loop Anti Join  (cost=0.56..14234.56 rows=11500 width=52) (actual time=1.248..3.225 rows=100 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=115 read=18 ->  Nested Loop  (cost=0.56..11345.11 rows=12000 width=52) (actual time=1.121..2.799 rows=102 loops=1) Buffers: shared hit=96 read=18 ->  Index Scan using orders_created_at_desc_inc_idx on orders o  (cost=0.29..8234.29 rows=120000 width=28) (actual time=0.072..0.924 rows=149 loops=1) Index Cond: (created_at >= (now() - '30 days'::interval)) Filter: (status = ANY ('{paid,shipped}'::text[])) Rows Removed by Filter: 47 Buffers: shared hit=50 read=18 ->  Index Scan using customers_pkey on customers c  (cost=0.27..0.03 rows=1 width=32) (actual time=0.012..0.012 rows=1 loops=149) Index Cond: (id = o.customer_id) Filter: is_active Rows Removed by Filter: 0 Buffers: shared hit=46 ->  Index Only Scan using refunds_completed_order_id_idx on refunds r  (cost=0.29..0.30 rows=1 width=4) (actual time=0.004..0.004 rows=0 loops=102) Index Cond: (order_id = o.id) Heap Fetches: 0 Buffers: shared hit=19 ->  Aggregate  (cost=0.43..0.44 rows=1 width=32) (actual time=0.578..0.578 rows=1 loops=100) Buffers: shared hit=800 read=94 ->  Index Scan using order_items_order_id_idx on order_items oi  (cost=0.42..0.43 rows=5 width=16) (actual time=0.054..0.356 rows=6 loops=100) Index Cond: (order_id = o.id) Buffers: shared hit=800 read=94 Planning Time: 1.102 ms Execution Time: 62.238 ms This is great. But why is there still a Sort at the top? Can we remove it?

The top sort is because the outer query does `ORDER BY o.created_at DESC`, but the planner may not be able to guarantee the rows remain in that order after the nested loop with the LATERAL aggregate. Even though nested loop often preserves order of the outer input, Postgres doesn’t always mark it as “ordered” depending on node types. However, since you only have 100 rows, that sort is trivial (37kB, ~0.02ms-ish inside the 62ms). Removing it won’t materially improve runtime. If you still want to try to eliminate it, a couple options: 1) Remove the outer ORDER BY and rely on the subquery’s ordering. In SQL, ordering is only guaranteed at the top level, not inside a subquery unless the top query orders. So strictly speaking you need the top ORDER BY to guarantee output order. But practically, many apps rely on it; not recommended. 2) Use a MATERIALIZED CTE and keep ordering: WITH recent AS MATERIALIZED ( ... ORDER BY ... LIMIT 100 ) SELECT ... FROM recent JOIN LATERAL ... ORDER BY recent.created_at DESC; This may allow the planner to keep the order property, but it still might sort. Also materialization is an extra step, but for 100 rows it’s negligible. 3) Add `ORDER BY` in the subquery and remove it outside, and accept that order is not guaranteed by the standard. Again, not recommended. Given the sort is tiny and the plan is now efficient, I’d keep it. If you want to shave a bit more: - Consider `order_items_order_id_inc_idx` to reduce heap reads if those 94 reads are costly. But 62ms is already good. Also verify correctness: because you limited to 100 orders after filtering, you’re good. Anything else you want to optimize—e.g., adding extra filters, pagination, or returning more columns?

Next problem: pagination. We need page 1..N of this report, 100 per page, sorted by created_at desc. OFFSET gets slow. How to do keyset pagination with this shape, especially with the NOT EXISTS and totals?

Use keyset (seek) pagination based on `(created_at, id)` as the cursor. Since created_at may not be unique, include id as a tiebreaker. Page 1: no cursor, just LIMIT 100. Next page: pass last row’s `(created_at, id)` and fetch rows “older than” that tuple. Core condition: WHERE (o.created_at, o.id) < (:cursor_created_at, :cursor_id) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC, o.id DESC LIMIT 100 You must align index with that order: CREATE INDEX orders_created_at_id_desc_idx ON orders (created_at DESC, id DESC) INCLUDE (customer_id, status); You can keep your existing created_at index, but adding id as second key helps for stable ordering and keyset. Then adapt the “recent orders” selection subquery: SELECT x.id, x.created_at, x.email, totals.total FROM ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE c.is_active AND o.created_at >= now() - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id=o.id AND r.status='completed' ) AND ( :cursor_created_at IS NULL OR (o.created_at, o.id) < (:cursor_created_at, :cursor_id) ) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC, o.id DESC LIMIT 100 ) x JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(oi.qty * oi.unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = x.id ) totals ON true ORDER BY x.created_at DESC, x.id DESC; For page 1, cursor params are NULL. This keeps the efficient top-N behavior: index scan can start from the top (or from the cursor position) and stop at 100 matches. Note: with keyset, you can’t jump to arbitrary page N without iterating, but it keeps performance stable. If you need “page N” direct access, you’ll need either: - precomputed snapshots / materialized view with row_number, or - store a denormalized reporting table. Also, ensure your anti-join and active customers filter remain in the subquery; they will be evaluated per candidate order. With a good index, you’ll only check a few hundred candidates per page.
For the cursor, should I use created_at from orders or from the result set after filters? Also, any caveats with now() - interval '30 days' moving between requests?

Use the `(created_at, id)` from the result row you returned (which comes from orders), so it’s consistent with your ORDER BY keys. Caveats: 1) Moving time window: If you use `created_at >= now() - interval '30 days'` on every request, the boundary shifts slightly between page fetches. That can cause: - missing rows (if something falls out of the 30-day window between requests), - duplicates (if new rows are inserted at the top between requests). Common fix: pin a “report_as_of” timestamp on page 1 and reuse it: - On first request, compute `:as_of = now()` in app (or SELECT now()). - Then use `created_at >= :as_of - interval '30 days'` for all subsequent pages. 2) Concurrent inserts: Even with pinned as_of, new orders inserted after as_of won’t appear. That’s usually desirable for stable pagination. 3) Updates to status/refunds: If an order’s status changes or a completed refund gets added between page requests, it may move in/out of the result set, causing duplicates/missing. If you need strict consistency, use a transaction snapshot (REPEATABLE READ) across paging (often impractical), or generate a snapshot table keyed by as_of. So recommended pagination parameters: - as_of timestamp (fixed) - cursor_created_at, cursor_id Query uses: o.created_at >= :as_of - interval '30 days' and cursor condition.
Makes sense. Another question: could we precompute totals to avoid 100 index scans into order_items per page? Some orders have 200+ items, and totals becomes heavy when exporting 10k rows.

Yes—once you move from “show 100 rows” to “export 10k+ rows”, per-order lateral aggregation becomes expensive (10k index probes + lots of heap work). For export/reporting, you typically want either: 1) A maintained summary table (incremental aggregation) Create `order_totals(order_id primary key, total numeric, updated_at ...)` and update it: - via triggers on order_items insert/update/delete, or - via async job / stream processing. Then the query becomes just a join to order_totals. 2) Materialized view refreshed periodically If near-real-time isn’t required: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW order_totals_mv AS SELECT order_id, SUM(qty*unit_price) AS total FROM order_items GROUP BY order_id; Index it on (order_id). Refresh concurrently if needed: REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY order_totals_mv; 3) Batch aggregation for export For one-off exports, compute totals for all candidate orders in one pass: - First fetch candidate order ids (maybe 10k) - Then join order_items with `WHERE order_id = ANY(:ids)` and GROUP BY order_id. This avoids 10k separate index scans, but can be memory-heavy; better is a temp table of ids and join. Example with temp table `tmp_order_ids`: INSERT ids... Then: SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty*oi.unit_price) FROM order_items oi JOIN tmp_order_ids t ON t.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id; This results in a hash join + group aggregate over only relevant items. If you truly need frequent export of large ranges, the summary table is best. Tradeoffs: - triggers add write overhead and complexity - async jobs can lag but reduce write latency - materialized views are simpler but refresh cost can be high If you share how “real-time” totals must be and write volume on order_items, we can pick a strategy.
Write volume is moderate: ~500 new orders/day, ~5k order_items/day. Totals need to be accurate within a minute. I’m OK with async jobs. Can you outline a robust design in Postgres?

Use an “incremental rollup” table updated via a queue table + worker. Tables: 1) order_totals - order_id PK references orders(id) - total numeric not null - updated_at timestamptz not null 2) order_total_recalc_queue - order_id PK (dedupe) - enqueued_at timestamptz not null default now() On any change to order_items affecting an order (insert/update/delete), enqueue the order_id: INSERT INTO order_total_recalc_queue(order_id) VALUES (NEW.order_id) ON CONFLICT (order_id) DO UPDATE SET enqueued_at = EXCLUDED.enqueued_at; Do the same for OLD.order_id on update where order_id changes, and on delete. Trigger function: - AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON order_items - Minimal work: just enqueue. Worker (every few seconds): - Atomically claim a batch: WITH claimed AS ( DELETE FROM order_total_recalc_queue WHERE order_id IN ( SELECT order_id FROM order_total_recalc_queue ORDER BY enqueued_at LIMIT 500 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED ) RETURNING order_id ) SELECT order_id FROM claimed; Then recompute totals for claimed order_ids in one query: INSERT INTO order_totals(order_id, total, updated_at) SELECT oi.order_id, COALESCE(SUM(oi.qty*oi.unit_price),0) AS total, now() FROM order_items oi JOIN claimed_ids c ON c.order_id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ON CONFLICT (order_id) DO UPDATE SET total = EXCLUDED.total, updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at; Implementation detail: claimed_ids can be a temporary table populated from claimed rows, or do it in a CTE chain: WITH claimed AS ( DELETE FROM order_total_recalc_queue WHERE order_id IN ( SELECT order_id FROM order_total_recalc_queue ORDER BY enqueued_at LIMIT 500 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED ) RETURNING order_id ), agg AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, COALESCE(SUM(oi.qty*oi.unit_price),0) AS total FROM order_items oi JOIN claimed c ON c.order_id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ) INSERT INTO order_totals(order_id, total, updated_at) SELECT order_id, total, now() FROM agg ON CONFLICT (order_id) DO UPDATE SET total=EXCLUDED.total, updated_at=EXCLUDED.updated_at; Edge cases: - Orders with zero items: if possible, ensure they exist in order_totals with total 0.
