The above agg won’t produce rows for zero-item orders; handle by left joining orders or inserting zeros for claimed order_ids not present. Option: WITH claimed AS (...), agg AS (...), zeros AS ( SELECT c.order_id, 0::numeric AS total FROM claimed c LEFT JOIN agg a ON a.order_id=c.order_id WHERE a.order_id IS NULL ), upsert AS ( SELECT * FROM agg UNION ALL SELECT * FROM zeros ) INSERT ... SELECT ... FROM upsert ... Indexes: - order_total_recalc_queue(order_id) PK, and maybe (enqueued_at) for ordering - order_items(order_id) already exists - order_totals(order_id) PK Then your report query uses order_totals: ... JOIN order_totals ot ON ot.order_id=o.id ... No lateral aggregation. This should comfortably keep within a minute given your volumes.
If we do that, could we drop the order_items_order_id_idx? Or is it still needed?

Keep it. Even if reports use order_totals, you’ll still need efficient access patterns: - The worker’s aggregation query groups by order_id and filters to a set of claimed order_ids. That join benefits from `order_items(order_id)` heavily. - Also other parts of the app might query items by order_id. So `order_items(order_id)` remains a core index. You might consider adding a composite index if you frequently filter within items (e.g., by product_id), but for totals the single-column index is fine.
Understood. One more: the anti-join on refunds. If we later need to exclude orders with any refund in statuses ('completed','processing'), can the partial index still work?

Yes, make the partial index match the exclusion predicate: Option A: partial index for those statuses: CREATE INDEX refunds_excluding_order_id_idx ON refunds(order_id) WHERE status IN ('completed','processing'); Then NOT EXISTS checks either status. Option B: general composite: CREATE INDEX refunds_order_id_status_idx ON refunds(order_id, status); General index is more flexible but larger. If the excluded statuses are rare and stable, partial is best. Query: AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id=o.id AND r.status IN ('completed','processing') ) Planner can use the partial index efficiently.
Great. Can we briefly discuss why the original plan chose seq scan on order_items even though there’s an index on order_id? I want to explain it to the team.

Because with the original query shape, the planner expected to join a large fraction of orders to items. Original plan: - `orders` filtered to ~480k rows. - Joining to `order_items` with `oi.order_id = o.id` yields ~2.9M rows. If it used an index nested loop from orders into order_items: - It would do ~480k index probes into `order_items(order_id)`. Even if each probe is fast, 480k random-ish accesses can be very expensive (CPU + random I/O), especially if order_items isn’t cached. So Postgres chose: - Seq scan all order_items once (3M rows), build hash table of orders (or items) and do a hash join. That’s often cheaper for large joins because it’s sequential I/O and fewer random lookups. The index isn’t “ignored”; it’s just not the cheapest path for the estimated row counts. After rewrite, the outer side is only 100 orders, so index nested loop becomes the cheapest by far. This is a classic “LIMIT can’t be pushed past aggregation/join” problem: the planner had to aggregate/sort after joining everything, so it couldn’t stop early.
Thanks. Final question: any Postgres settings worth adjusting? work_mem? shared_buffers? Or is this purely query/index?

For your improved query (62ms), settings likely aren’t necessary. For the original query, more work_mem might have reduced hash aggregate spill risk, but your plan showed HashAggregate in one batch with ~101MB memory, so it already fit (or at least didn’t spill). The bigger cost was scanning and joining millions of rows. General guidance: - work_mem: affects sorts/hashes per node. If you do large exports with big GROUP BY and see “Disk” in sort method or hash batches > 1, increasing work_mem for that session can help. - shared_buffers: helps caching, but tuning depends on system RAM and workload; not a quick fix. - effective_cache_size: informs planner about cache; can affect plan choice. - random_page_cost / seq_page_cost: if on SSD, lowering random_page_cost can make index nested loops more attractive, but you don’t want to force bad plans globally. Given you solved it structurally, I’d keep defaults and only consider per-session work_mem for heavy export jobs. If you later do the “aggregate 10k orders” approach, you may need more work_mem to hash aggregate without spilling.
Perfect. That covers it.

We implemented the order_totals + queue approach. It works, but occasionally totals are stale for a few minutes. I think the worker sometimes falls behind. How can we monitor lag and make the system self-healing?

Add explicit observability and backpressure controls around the queue and worker, so you can measure “how far behind” you are and scale up workers safely. 1) Measure lag - Queue depth: SELECT count(*) FROM order_total_recalc_queue; - Oldest enqueue time (worst-case lag signal): SELECT now() - min(enqueued_at) AS oldest_lag FROM order_total_recalc_queue; - Distribution (to see if there’s a long tail): SELECT width_bucket(extract(epoch from (now()-enqueued_at)), 0, 600, 6) AS bucket, count(*) FROM order_total_recalc_queue GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1; 2) Measure “freshness” of totals table If every order that changes gets enqueued, then a stale total should correlate with queue lag. Still, you can add: - updated_at in order_totals (you already have it). For a given order: SELECT now() - updated_at FROM order_totals WHERE order_id = $1; 3) Self-healing tactics A) Run multiple workers concurrently Your claiming query using SKIP LOCKED supports parallel workers. Run 2–4 workers if backlog grows. The critical part is ensuring each worker: - claims disjoint sets (SKIP LOCKED does that) - recomputes totals idempotently (upsert does that) B) Adaptive batch size If you have spikes (e.g., bulk edits to items), increase LIMIT from 500 to e.g. 2000, but only if each batch finishes quickly. A good pattern: - aim for batches that finish in < 1 second - adjust batch size dynamically based on last runtime (in the worker code) C) Safety “sweeper” job Once per hour (or day), run a job that looks for suspiciously old totals and forces recalculation, in case some enqueue was missed due to a bug or a failed transaction: - If you have an order_items.updated_at, you can detect mismatch: - store order_items_last_changed_at per order (hard unless you track it) Simpler: periodic full repair for recent orders: INSERT INTO order_total_recalc_queue(order_id) SELECT id FROM orders WHERE created_at >= now() - interval '7 days' ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; This is crude but effective for moderate volumes. D) Ensure the trigger is minimal and reliable - Use AFTER triggers (not BEFORE). - Ensure it always enqueues both OLD and NEW order_id on updates that can move items between orders. - Keep trigger function tiny; no aggregation in triggers. 4) Failure modes to check - Worker crashes after claiming but before upsert: since you DELETE from queue to claim, those order_ids would be lost. Fix by “claiming” via UPDATE (mark claimed) rather than DELETE, or by deleting only after successful upsert. Robust pattern: - Add columns: claimed_at, claimed_by, attempts - Claim via UPDATE ... WHERE claimed_at IS NULL ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED RETURNING - After successful upsert, DELETE claimed rows. This makes it resilient to worker crashes; a watchdog can release stuck claims. Example claim: WITH c AS ( SELECT order_id FROM order_total_recalc_queue WHERE claimed_at IS NULL ORDER BY enqueued_at LIMIT 500 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED ) UPDATE order_total_recalc_queue q SET claimed_at = now(), claimed_by = $worker_id, attempts = attempts + 1 FROM c WHERE q.order_id = c.order_id RETURNING q.order_id; Then upsert totals for returned ids. Then: DELETE FROM order_total_recalc_queue WHERE order_id = ANY($claimed_ids); And a reaper: UPDATE order_total_recalc_queue SET claimed_at = NULL, claimed_by = NULL WHERE claimed_at < now() - interval '2 minutes'; That’s the “self-healing” part. If you want, paste your current queue table definition + worker SQL and I can adjust it to the crash-safe version you can run with multiple workers.

Good point about losing claimed rows. We currently DELETE ... RETURNING then compute totals, so a crash could drop work. Let’s change to the claimed_at approach. Any indexing needed on the queue table?

Yes; you want the claim query to be fast and avoid scanning the whole queue. Queue table: - Primary key on (order_id) is fine for dedupe. - Add an index to support “unclaimed oldest first”: CREATE INDEX order_total_recalc_queue_unclaimed_enqueued_idx ON order_total_recalc_queue (enqueued_at) WHERE claimed_at IS NULL; This partial index keeps it small and directly supports: WHERE claimed_at IS NULL ORDER BY enqueued_at LIMIT ... If you also filter by claimed_at for the reaper: - The reaper can use: CREATE INDEX order_total_recalc_queue_claimed_at_idx ON order_total_recalc_queue (claimed_at) WHERE claimed_at IS NOT NULL; But if the queue is small/moderate, you might not need the second index. The first one is the key. Also ensure attempts has a default 0 and claimed_at is nullable.

After switching, EXPLAIN on the worker aggregation shows a HashAggregate over order_items, sometimes spilling to disk when a batch includes a few huge orders. Should we change the worker query to avoid that?

Two approaches: 1) Keep batch size but aggregate per order with LATERAL (avoids big hash) Instead of aggregating all claimed orders in one GROUP BY (which can build a large hash table and spill), do per-order aggregation using a lateral subquery. That trades one big hash aggregate for N smaller index scans. With a batch size of 500, that’s 500 index scans—often fine, but if you have huge orders, each scan is still bounded to that order’s items and won’t spill. Pattern: WITH claimed AS ( ... RETURNING order_id ) INSERT INTO order_totals(order_id, total, updated_at) SELECT c.order_id, COALESCE(t.total,0), now() FROM claimed c LEFT JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(qty*unit_price) AS total FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = c.order_id ) t ON true ON CONFLICT (order_id) DO UPDATE ... This tends to be stable and spill-free, provided order_items(order_id) is indexed (it is). 2) Keep set-based aggregation, but cap batch size and raise work_mem per worker If you prefer set-based for throughput: - Reduce claim batch size (e.g., 200) - In the worker session, set a higher work_mem: SET LOCAL work_mem = '256MB'; This only affects that worker connection, not global. Which is better depends on your data shape: - If most orders have few items, set-based aggregation is fastest. - If you have a long tail of huge orders that blow up memory, lateral per-order is more predictable. Given you’re already okay with 1-minute accuracy and write volume is low, I’d favor predictability: use LATERAL per claimed order and keep batch size moderate (e.g., 200–1000 depending on runtime).
If we use LATERAL in the worker, do we still need the INCLUDE index on order_items? We only have order_items(order_id).

You don’t need INCLUDE for correctness. Performance-wise: - With LATERAL, each order triggers an index scan on order_items(order_id), and Postgres will still need to read qty and unit_price from the heap unless the index includes them and an index-only scan is possible. - But index-only scans require visibility map coverage; on a frequently updated table, you might still hit heap. Given your write volume is small (5k items/day), visibility map should be decent after autovacuum, so INCLUDE can help somewhat. Still, it’s optional. I’d decide based on what you see in BUFFERS: - If you see lots of heap reads for the worker and it matters, add: CREATE INDEX order_items_order_id_inc_idx ON order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, unit_price); Downside: extra index size and write overhead (small in your case). For reporting-heavy systems, it’s usually worth it.

Switching topics slightly: our orders table is growing fast (tens of millions long-term). The report always uses “last 30 days”. Should we partition orders by month? Would that make the index scan even faster?

Partitioning can help primarily by: - keeping indexes smaller per partition - letting the planner prune old partitions for time-range queries For your “top-N last 30 days” query: - With a good btree on (created_at desc, id desc), Postgres already does a tight index scan and stops quickly. Partitioning won’t make that part dramatically faster for page 1, because it’s already reading ~hundreds of index entries. - Partitioning helps more for: - exports over the full 30 days (scanning many rows) - maintenance (vacuum, reindex) and retention (dropping old partitions) - keeping table bloat under control If you expect tens of millions and you have a clear retention policy or frequent range scans, monthly partitioning by created_at is reasonable. Caveats: - Partitioning adds complexity: constraints, indexes per partition, and query plans can get more complex. - You must ensure all queries include created_at predicates for pruning; your report does, but other queries might not. If you do partition: - Use RANGE partitioning on created_at by month. - Create local indexes on each partition: (created_at DESC, id DESC) INCLUDE (customer_id, status) - Consider also partitioning order_items by order_id or by created_at via join to orders? That’s harder. Often you leave order_items unpartitioned and rely on order_id index. For your current report, partitioning is more about long-term manageability and bulk export speed than making the first page faster.
We also have a query that filters orders by customer_id and created_at range, like “show a customer’s last 1 year orders”, sorted by created_at desc. With partitioning, do we need different indexes?

Yes, you’d likely want a customer-centric index too. Without partitioning, a strong index is: CREATE INDEX orders_customer_created_at_id_desc_idx ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC, id DESC) INCLUDE (status); This supports: WHERE customer_id = ? AND created_at >= ? ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC LIMIT ... With partitioning by created_at: - You’d create that same index on each partition (local index). It still helps because within each partition, customer_id narrows the search. But note: for “last 1 year”, that spans 12 partitions. The planner will query multiple partitions and merge results. With ORDER BY + LIMIT, it can do an Append + MergeAppend plan that pulls the newest rows from the newest partitions first, but it may still touch multiple partitions depending on how many orders the customer has. If per-customer order volume is low, it’s fine. If high, the composite index is important.
One more optimization: in the report query, we join customers just to get email and filter is_active. Email is immutable. Would denormalizing email into orders help?

It can, but weigh it: Pros: - Avoids join to customers in report. - Makes the “recent orders selection” cheaper and more index-friendly (one less nested loop lookup). Cons: - Data duplication and update complexity if email ever changes (even if “immutable”, reality often changes). - Potential compliance/audit implications (PII duplicated in more places). Given your optimized plan already does ~149 PK lookups into customers to find 100 rows—trivial. For exports of 10k rows, it’s 10k lookups, still usually fine. So I wouldn’t denormalize just for performance unless: - customers lookup becomes a measurable bottleneck, or - you need historical email-as-of-order-time (which is a legitimate business reason). If you need historical “email at time of order”, then denormalizing into orders is correct semantically, not just an optimization.
Fair. For the report, we also need to show “has_refund” boolean (any refund at all) and “has_completed_refund” boolean. How do we add those without killing performance?

Since you already have the anti-join for completed refunds, you can compute both flags using EXISTS checks, but do it only for the 100 selected orders (or for paged rows). That keeps it cheap. In the “recent orders” subquery (the part that selects 100 orders), add: - has_completed_refund: EXISTS(...) with status='completed' - has_refund: EXISTS(...) without status filter But note: you already exclude completed refunds in the report. If you still exclude them, has_completed_refund will always be false. If you’re changing requirements to include them but flag them, then remove the NOT EXISTS filter. Assuming new requirement is: include all orders, but show flags. Then: SELECT x.id, x.created_at, x.email, totals.total, x.has_refund, x.has_completed_refund FROM ( SELECT o.id, o.created_at, c.email, EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id=o.id) AS has_refund, EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM refunds r WHERE r.order_id=o.id AND r.status='completed') AS has_completed_refund FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id=o.customer_id WHERE c.is_active AND o.created_at >= :as_of - interval '30 days' AND o.status IN ('paid','shipped') AND (:cursor_created_at IS NULL OR (o.created_at, o.id) < (:cursor_created_at, :cursor_id)) ORDER BY o.created_at DESC, o.id DESC LIMIT 100 ) x JOIN order_totals ot ON ot.order_id = x.id ORDER BY x.created_at DESC, x.id DESC; Indexes: - refunds(order_id) already exists: supports has_refund EXISTS - refunds partial on completed supports has_completed_refund EXISTS Both are executed only 100 times per page, so fast. If you still need to exclude completed refunds AND also show has_refund (which could be true due to pending refunds), keep NOT EXISTS(completed) and include EXISTS(any) as a separate flag.
For refunds, there can be many rows per order (audit trail). EXISTS will still scan until it finds one. Should we add a covering index to avoid heap access?

For EXISTS, Postgres can use an index-only scan if: - the index contains all needed columns (for EXISTS it only needs order_id and maybe status) - visibility map allows it (no heap fetch) So: - For has_refund: index on refunds(order_id) is enough. - For has_completed_refund: your partial index on (order_id) WHERE status='completed' is ideal. Both can be index-only if the table is vacuumed enough. If you see heap fetches, you can: - ensure autovacuum is keeping up (analyze/vacuum) - consider lowering autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor for refunds if it changes frequently But adding INCLUDE columns doesn’t help much for EXISTS because you’re not selecting additional columns.
Autovacuum tuning then: refunds and order_items are append-heavy. Any baseline settings you recommend per table?

For append-heavy tables, the goal is to keep visibility maps healthy (for index-only scans) and keep bloat low without vacuuming too aggressively. Per-table settings you can set via ALTER TABLE ... SET ( ... ): For order_items (5k/day, some updates maybe): - If mostly inserts, default autovacuum is often fine. If you want more aggressive analyze (to keep planner stats fresh): ALTER TABLE order_items SET ( autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.02, autovacuum_analyze_threshold = 2000 ); For refunds (audit trail, likely inserts only): - Similar: ALTER TABLE refunds SET ( autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.02, autovacuum_analyze_threshold = 500 ); Vacuum thresholds matter more if you have updates/deletes. If truly insert-only, vacuum isn’t triggered by inserts; visibility map won’t be set for new pages until vacuum runs, so index-only scans may still need heap fetches for the newest pages. That’s normal. If index-only performance matters a lot, you can schedule manual VACUUM (FREEZE) or rely on autovacuum’s anti-wraparound vacuums. If you do have updates/deletes (e.g., corrections), set lower vacuum scale factors too: autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.05 autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 5000 Tune based on table size and churn. Before changing, check: - pg_stat_user_tables: n_dead_tup, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze - pg_stat_all_tables: vacuum/analyze counts
Ok. Another performance issue: sometimes the report query slows from 60ms to 400ms when the cache is cold after deploy. Any strategies to warm cache or reduce dependency on caching?

A few pragmatic options: 1) Reduce random I/O by making the query more index-friendly You already did: top-N orders via created_at index, and order_totals join avoids touching order_items. That’s the biggest lever. 2) Prewarm critical indexes After deploy/restart, you can run: - pg_prewarm extension (if allowed): CREATE EXTENSION pg_prewarm; SELECT pg_prewarm('orders_created_at_desc_inc_idx'); SELECT pg_prewarm('refunds_completed_order_id_idx'); SELECT pg_prewarm('order_totals_pkey'); (or the table) This pulls blocks into shared_buffers (not OS cache necessarily, but helps). Without pg_prewarm, you can “touch” data with a lightweight query: - Run the report query once (or a simplified version) as a warmup job. 3) Increase shared_buffers / effective_cache_size This is system-level tuning; it can help but needs careful sizing. Not a deploy-time quick fix. 4) Use connection pooling and avoid restarting DB If deploy restarts app only, DB cache should remain warm. If you restart DB frequently, you’ll always pay cold-cache penalties. 5) Keep working set small Partitioning can help keep hot partitions and their indexes smaller, improving cache residency. 400ms cold-cache for a query that touches ~100–1000 blocks isn’t unusual; if it’s acceptable, you might leave it. If it’s user-facing and needs to be consistent, prewarm is the most direct.
We can enable pg_prewarm. Should we prewarm tables or indexes, and which ones?

Prewarm indexes first; they’re smaller and directly impact your access paths. For the report: - orders_created_at... index (and possibly the heap for the newest pages) - order_totals primary key index + maybe the table - refunds partial index If you prewarm only one thing: orders created_at index. Example: SELECT pg_prewarm('orders_created_at_desc_inc_idx', 'buffer'); SELECT pg_prewarm('order_totals', 'buffer'); SELECT pg_prewarm('refunds_completed_order_id_idx', 'buffer'); Mode 'buffer' warms shared_buffers. There’s also 'prefetch' to ask the OS to read ahead. Note: prewarming huge objects can evict other useful cache; keep it targeted.
Last thing: we noticed the orders_created_at_desc_inc_idx got big. Is INCLUDE(status) worth it? Status is small text. Could we change it to an enum or smallint and shrink index?

INCLUDE(status) increases index size because it stores the status value in leaf tuples. Whether it’s worth it depends on whether you’re getting index-only scans and whether heap fetches are a bottleneck. Given your plan earlier: - Index Scan on orders with Filter on status. Even without INCLUDE(status), Postgres can still use the index to find recent rows; it would just need to visit the heap to check status (unless status is in the index keys or included). With cold cache, those heap hits can cost. Options: 1) Keep INCLUDE(status) if it measurably reduces heap fetches and improves latency consistency. 2) If index bloat/size is a concern, drop INCLUDE(status) and instead make status a key column: - (created_at desc, status, id desc) doesn’t help ORDER BY created_at alone. - (status, created_at desc, id desc) helps filtering but complicates global ordering across statuses (paid/shipped). It might still be fine if you accept a small sort/merge step. 3) Change status datatype If status is TEXT with a few values, switching to: - an enum, or - a smallint referencing a lookup table will reduce both heap and index size (including included columns), and comparisons are faster. But datatype migration has app impact. If you’re already planning schema evolution and status is stable, enum/smallint is reasonable. Minimal-change approach: - Keep TEXT, but consider not including it if you don’t need index-only scans. Measure: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) and check Heap Fetches and read buffers for orders. If heap fetches are low anyway (because you only scan ~150 rows to get 100), then INCLUDE(status) probably isn’t worth index growth. If you want, share: - index size (pg_relation_size) - average rows scanned to produce 100 and we can decide whether to rebuild the index without INCLUDE(status).
What's the difference between a Docker image and a container? I keep conflating them.

A Docker image is a read-only template (a packaged filesystem plus metadata) that describes what a container should run: which files it has, what command it starts with, environment defaults, exposed ports, etc. A container is a running (or stopped) instance created from an image—like a process with its own isolated filesystem layer, network namespace, and resource limits. A simple mental model: - Image = recipe + ingredients snapshot (immutable) - Container = the dish you made from the recipe (mutable while it exists) Key practical differences: - Images are built (from a Dockerfile) and stored in an image cache/registry; containers are created and started. - Many containers can be created from the same image. - Containers have a writable layer on top of the image layers; changes inside the container go into that writable layer (unless you use volumes/bind mounts).
So if I run `docker run ubuntu`, am I downloading an image or creating a container?

`docker run ubuntu` does both (if needed). Step-by-step: 1) Docker checks whether the `ubuntu` image exists locally. 2) If not, it pulls the image from a registry (by default Docker Hub). 3) It creates a new container from that image. 4) It starts the container (runs the default command in the image). So: it may download an image, and it definitely creates a container.
Where does the command come from? Like sometimes `docker run ubuntu` exits immediately.

The image has a default command specified in its metadata—coming from the Dockerfile instructions `CMD` and/or `ENTRYPOINT`. For the `ubuntu` image, the default is typically something like `bash` (or sometimes nothing meaningful for an interactive session). If you run `docker run ubuntu` without `-it`, Docker starts the default command, but since there’s no interactive terminal, it may exit quickly (or appear to do nothing) depending on the command and whether it expects a TTY. Try: - `docker run -it ubuntu bash` to start an interactive shell. - Or `docker run --rm -it ubuntu` (which uses the default command and removes the container when it exits). The key point: containers run a process; when that main process exits, the container stops.
Wait, so a container is basically just a process?

Conceptually, yes: a container is a process (or process tree) running on the host, with isolation boundaries (namespaces) and resource constraints (cgroups), plus a layered filesystem (union filesystem). But people say “a container” as shorthand for: - the runtime configuration (env vars, mounts, network), - the filesystem layers (image layers + writable layer), - and the running process. At the kernel level, it’s not a full VM; it’s the host kernel running processes with isolation.
If it’s just a process, why do people treat it like a mini-computer?

Because the isolation makes it feel like one: - It can have its own filesystem view (it sees `/` from the image + writable layer). - It can have its own PID namespace (so process IDs start from 1 inside). - It can have its own network stack (interfaces, IP, ports). - It can have its own users/groups (user namespace, if enabled). So it resembles a machine from the inside, but it’s still sharing the host kernel.
Okay. Then what exactly is inside an image? Is it like an ISO?

It’s closer to a snapshot of a filesystem plus some metadata, but not a bootable disk image like an ISO. An image includes: - A set of filesystem layers (each produced by a build step). - Metadata: default command, entrypoint, environment variables, working directory, labels, exposed ports. - A config JSON that references the layers and describes how to run. The layers are typically tar archives stored in Docker’s storage backend (overlay2 is common on Linux). When a container starts, Docker mounts these layers together into a single filesystem view.
You said layers come from build steps. Like each Dockerfile line becomes a layer?

Not each line, but many instructions create layers. Generally: - `RUN`, `COPY`, and `ADD` create filesystem layers. - Some instructions only change metadata (no new filesystem layer): `CMD`, `ENTRYPOINT`, `ENV`, `WORKDIR`, `EXPOSE`, `LABEL`, `USER`, etc. So if your Dockerfile is: - `FROM python:3.12` - `WORKDIR /app` - `COPY . .` - `RUN pip install -r requirements.txt` - `CMD ["python","main.py"]` The `COPY` and `RUN` typically add layers; `WORKDIR` and `CMD` are metadata changes.
Why does layering matter?

Layering matters for caching and reuse. Benefits: - Build cache: If earlier layers haven’t changed, Docker can reuse them, making rebuilds faster. - Sharing: Multiple images can share layers. If two images are based on the same base image, they share those layers on disk. - Pull efficiency: When pulling an image, Docker downloads only layers it doesn’t already have. Downside: - If you add a big file in a layer and remove it in a later layer, the earlier layer still contains it; the overall image can stay large.
I’ve seen `docker images` and `docker ps`. That’s images vs containers?

Exactly: - `docker images` lists images stored locally (and their tags). - `docker ps` lists running containers. - `docker ps -a` lists all containers (including stopped). The relationship: an image can exist without any containers. A container always references an image (the image it was created from), but it also has its own writable layer and runtime config.
If I delete an image, do the containers break?

It depends on what you mean by “delete.” - If you remove an image that has existing containers referencing it, Docker usually prevents the removal unless you force it. You might see an error like “image is being used by running container” or “by a stopped container.” - If you force-remove it, the container can still exist because it has already been created with its own writable layer and it references the image layers. But Docker’s bookkeeping may make it awkward; typically you keep the image around as long as containers exist. In general: don’t remove images that have containers you care about. Remove containers first, then remove images.
What’s the point of stopped containers? Aren’t they useless?

Stopped containers can still be useful because they preserve: - their writable filesystem layer (changes made inside), - some metadata (like logs depending on logging driver), - and they can be restarted (`docker start containername`) without re-creating. But many workflows treat containers as disposable. That’s why `--rm` exists: it removes the container when it exits, encouraging “stateless” usage. If you need persistent data, you typically use volumes/bind mounts rather than relying on a container’s writable layer.
So if I `apt-get install` something inside a running container, that change isn’t in the image, right?

Correct. Installing packages inside a running container modifies the container’s writable layer, not the image. If you later create a new container from the same image, it won’t have those packages. To bake it into the image, you’d put the install step in the Dockerfile (`RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y ...`) and rebuild the image.
Then how do people “save” a container with changes?

There are a few approaches, each with tradeoffs: 1) Rebuild properly via Dockerfile (preferred) - Put the changes in a Dockerfile and build an image. This is reproducible. 2) `docker commit` (quick but not ideal) - `docker commit <container> myimage:tag` creates a new image from the container’s current filesystem state. - It’s not very reproducible, and it can capture accidental state. 3) Export/import - `docker export` exports a container filesystem as a tar (no history/layers). - `docker import` imports it as a new image (but loses metadata like CMD unless you set it). If you’re learning, `docker commit` can be a useful experiment to see the relationship, but for real repeatable setups, use Dockerfiles.
You mentioned volumes. How do they fit into images vs containers?

Volumes are separate from the image and separate from the container’s writable layer. - Image: read-only layers (packaged app) - Container: writable layer (ephemeral changes) - Volume (or bind mount): persistent storage mounted into the container at runtime If you store data in a volume, you can remove and recreate containers without losing that data. Example: `docker run -v mydata:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres` Here, the database files live in the `mydata` volume on the host (managed by Docker), not inside the container layer.
Bind mount vs volume—what’s the difference?

Both mount host storage into the container, but they differ in management and typical use: - Bind mount: maps a specific host path into the container. Example: `-v /home/me/project:/app` Good for development (live-edit files on host). Less portable (depends on host path existing). - Named volume: Docker-managed storage location. Example: `-v mydata:/data` Good for persistent app data. More portable; Docker manages where it lives. There are also “anonymous volumes” (no explicit name), which are created automatically but can be harder to manage.
When I run a container, is it copying the whole image into it?

Not in the naive “copy everything” sense. With a layered filesystem (like overlay2), Docker mounts the image layers as read-only and adds a thin writable layer for the container. It’s more like: - Base layers are shared and not duplicated per container. - Each container gets its own writable “diff” layer. So creating a container is fast and disk-efficient, because it doesn’t copy all files.
Could two containers from the same image see each other’s changes?

Not through their writable layers. Each container has its own writable layer, so changes inside one container’s filesystem don’t appear in another. But they can share data if: - they mount the same volume or bind mount, - they communicate over the network, - or they use some external service. So by default, filesystem changes are isolated per container.
How do tags fit in? Like `ubuntu:latest`.

A tag is a human-friendly pointer to an image version within a repository name. - `ubuntu` is the repository name. - `latest` is the tag. `ubuntu:latest` is just a default tag if you omit one. It does not necessarily mean “most recent stable”; it’s just a tag maintained by image publishers. Under the hood, tags point to image manifests (and ultimately image digests). The immutable identifier is the digest, like: `ubuntu@sha256:...` Tags can move (be retagged), digests don’t.
So if I want reproducibility, I should use the digest?

Yes. Using a digest pins the exact content. Example: `docker pull ubuntu@sha256:<digest>` Using tags can change over time (even `1.2.3` tags sometimes get rebuilt, though good projects avoid that). For learning, tags are fine; for strict reproducibility, pin digests (and/or use lockfiles in build systems).
I’ve heard of “dangling images.” What are those?

Dangling images are images that: - have no tag pointing to them (tag is `<none>`), - often created during builds when a new image replaces an old tag. They can accumulate and take disk space. You can list them: `docker images -f dangling=true` And remove unused objects with: - `docker image prune` (removes dangling images) - `docker system prune` (more aggressive; removes unused containers, networks, images, build cache—be careful)
What’s the build cache exactly?

During `docker build`, each step can be cached. Docker keys the cache based on: - the instruction itself, - the content of files it depends on (for `COPY`/`ADD`), - and the previous layer. If nothing relevant changed, Docker can reuse the previously built layer instead of re-running the step. Example: If you `COPY requirements.txt` and run `pip install`, then later change only your app code, you can structure the Dockerfile so the `pip install` layer stays cached. A common pattern: 1) Copy dependency manifest (requirements/package-lock) 2) Install dependencies 3) Copy the rest of the source
I’m confused about `COPY . .` and why it breaks caching.

Because `COPY . .` depends on everything in your build context (the directory you run `docker build` from, minus `.dockerignore`). If any file changes, the checksum changes, and Docker invalidates that layer and all subsequent layers. To improve caching: - Copy only dependency files first (like `requirements.txt`, `package.json`). - Install deps. - Then copy the rest. Also use `.dockerignore` to exclude things like `.git`, build artifacts, node_modules, etc., so changes there don’t invalidate cache and they don’t bloat the image.
Build context… that’s another term I don’t get.

The build context is the set of files sent to the Docker daemon for the build. When you run: `docker build -t myimg .` The `.` is the build context directory. Docker will package up that directory (respecting `.dockerignore`) and send it to the daemon. The Dockerfile can only `COPY` or `ADD` files from that context (unless you use advanced BuildKit features, but that’s later). So if your Dockerfile tries `COPY ../something /app`, it won’t work because it’s outside the context.
Why does Docker “send” files? Isn’t it local?

Historically, Docker used a daemon model, and the client and daemon could be on different machines. Even locally, it still follows a client-server pattern, so the client sends the build context to the daemon. With BuildKit and newer features, there are more efficient transfer mechanisms, but the mental model “context is what the daemon can see for COPY” still holds.
Okay. Back to containers: what happens when I `docker run` multiple times? Do I get multiple containers?

Yes. Each `docker run` creates a new container (with a new container ID and name unless you specify `--name`). Example: - `docker run -d nginx` (creates container A) - `docker run -d nginx` (creates container B) Both are separate instances from the same image. If you want to start an existing stopped container again, you use `docker start <name>`.
That explains why I have so many stopped containers. How do I clean them?

Options: - Remove a specific container: `docker rm <container>` - Remove all stopped containers: `docker container prune` - Remove everything unused (careful): `docker system prune` If you often run one-off containers, adding `--rm` to `docker run` automatically deletes the container after it exits.
What’s the difference between `docker rm` and `docker rmi`?
`docker rm` removes containers. `docker rmi` removes images. Mnemonic: - rm = remove container object - rmi = remove image
I tried to `docker rmi ubuntu` and it complained that it’s in use. But I didn’t have any running containers.

It can still be “in use” by stopped containers. Check: `docker ps -a --filter ancestor=ubuntu` If you see containers (even exited ones) created from `ubuntu`, remove them first: `docker rm <container-id>` Then you can remove the image: `docker rmi ubuntu` Alternatively, `docker rm -f` forces removal of a running container, but use it carefully.
What happens if I remove a container? Does that delete the data inside it?

It deletes the container’s writable layer and its configuration, so any data stored only inside the container filesystem is lost. But it does not delete: - images (they remain), - named volumes (unless you explicitly remove them), - bind-mounted files on the host (they’re just host files). If a container has an anonymous volume, removal may or may not remove it depending on flags. `docker rm -v` removes associated anonymous volumes. Named volumes generally persist until removed with `docker volume rm` or `docker volume prune`.
Can you explain container networking a bit? Like how ports work with `-p`.

Sure. By default, containers run in an isolated network namespace. They can make outbound connections, but inbound connections from your host to the container require publishing ports. `-p hostPort:containerPort` publishes a container port to the host. Example: `docker run -p 8080:80 nginx` - Nginx listens inside container on port 80. - Docker sets up port forwarding so your host’s port 8080 maps to the container’s port 80. - You access it via `http://localhost:8080`. If you do `-p 80` without specifying host port, Docker chooses a random host port.
What does `EXPOSE 80` do then? Is that publishing?

`EXPOSE` is documentation/metadata inside the image that says “this container expects to listen on port 80.” It does not publish the port to the host by itself. Publishing happens at runtime with `-p` or `--publish`, or in Compose with `ports:`. So: - `EXPOSE` = intent - `-p` = actual host mapping
I’ve seen `--network host`. Is that like disabling networking isolation?

Pretty much. `--network host` makes the container share the host’s network namespace (on Linux). That means: - No port mapping needed; container listens directly on host interfaces. - But you lose network isolation. - Ports can conflict with host services. On macOS/Windows (Docker Desktop), host networking behaves differently due to the VM layer. For learning, default bridge networking is fine.
Speaking of macOS/Windows, are containers still “just processes” there?

On Docker Desktop (macOS/Windows), containers run inside a lightweight Linux VM because the host kernel isn’t Linux. So: - Inside the VM, containers are just processes using Linux namespaces/cgroups. - From the macOS/Windows perspective, there’s a VM process running Linux. So the “containers share the host kernel” statement is true relative to the Linux kernel they run on, which may be in a VM.
I keep hearing about containerd, runc, OCI… are those relevant to images vs containers?

They’re part of the stack. - OCI (Open Container Initiative) defines standards for image format and runtime. - OCI Image Spec: how images are packaged (layers, config). - OCI Runtime Spec: how to run a container (create namespaces/cgroups, etc.). - runc: a low-level OCI runtime that actually creates the container process. - containerd: a higher-level runtime that manages container lifecycle, images, etc. Docker uses containerd under the hood. For basic usage, you don’t need to think about them, but it helps to know Docker isn’t magic—it’s using standardized components.
So an image is OCI format?

Most Docker images you pull from registries follow the OCI image format or Docker’s v2 schema, which is effectively compatible in practice. Many tools treat them interchangeably. Registries store manifests and layers; clients pull them and unpack into a local storage driver.
How does `docker save` fit in?

`docker save` exports one or more images (including their layers and metadata) into a tar archive. Example: `docker save -o ubuntu.tar ubuntu:latest` Then on another machine: `docker load -i ubuntu.tar` This is image-level export/import (preserves layers and tags). It differs from `docker export` which exports a container filesystem snapshot (flattened, no history).
If I want to move a running container to another machine, can I?

Not directly in a “live migrate” sense with plain Docker. Typical approaches: - Recreate the container on the other machine from the same image and configuration (Compose helps). - Persist data in volumes and move the volume data (or use external storage). - If you really need stateful migration, you’re in more advanced territory (checkpoint/restore with CRIU exists but isn’t common in everyday Docker workflows and has limitations). Most container usage assumes you can recreate containers rather than move them.
What is Docker Compose then? Is it about images or containers?

Compose is a tool to define and run multi-container setups via a YAML file (`compose.yaml` or `docker-compose.yml`). It orchestrates: - building or pulling images, - creating networks and volumes, - creating containers with the right environment variables, ports, dependencies. So it deals with both images and containers, but it’s mostly about defining container configurations and how they connect.
If I write a Compose file with `build: .`, does it create an image?

Yes. `build: .` tells Compose to build an image from a Dockerfile in that directory (unless you specify another path). Compose will build it and then create containers from it. If you also specify `image: myname:tag`, Compose will tag the built image with that name. Example: services: app: build: . image: myapp:dev
What does Compose do if I change the Dockerfile?

If you run `docker compose up --build`, it rebuilds images as needed (respecting cache) and recreates containers if the image changed or the service config changed. If you just run `docker compose up` without `--build`, it may use existing images and containers depending on what changed. Compose tracks the configuration hash; changes in environment, ports, mounts, etc. can trigger container recreation.
What does “recreate” mean exactly?

It means Compose removes the old container and creates a new one with the updated configuration (and possibly a new image). Because a container’s configuration (like port mappings, mounts) can’t always be changed in place. Volumes typically persist across recreation, so your data can survive if it’s in a volume.
I’m trying to understand why containers are considered “immutable” but also “mutable.” It seems contradictory.

The common “immutable infrastructure” idea is a practice, not a hard technical constraint. Technically: - A running container’s filesystem is mutable (you can write files, install packages). - The image is immutable (its layers don’t change). Practically: - People treat containers as disposable and avoid making manual changes inside them. - Instead, they rebuild the image to change the software. So “containers should be immutable” means “don’t rely on in-place mutations; rebuild and redeploy.”
If I exec into a container and edit a file, that’s bad practice?

It’s fine for debugging or learning. It becomes risky if: - you rely on that change being there later (because container might be recreated), - you can’t reproduce the change, - it diverges from your Dockerfile. A good workflow: - Use `docker exec` to inspect/debug. - Then make the real change in the Dockerfile or in your source code and rebuild.
Can you explain `docker exec` vs `docker attach`?

`docker exec` runs a new process inside an existing container. Example: `docker exec -it mycontainer bash` `docker attach` attaches your terminal to the container’s main process stdin/stdout/stderr. Example: `docker attach mycontainer` `exec` is usually safer for interactive debugging. `attach` can be awkward because if you exit or send signals you might stop the main process, depending on how it’s configured.
How do I see what image a container was created from?

Use: - `docker ps --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}"` or inspect: - `docker inspect <container> --format '{{.Config.Image}}'` `docker inspect` gives a lot of info: mounts, env vars, networks, entrypoint, etc.
And how do I see the layers of an image?

You can use: - `docker history <image>` to see the build history (commands) and layer sizes. - `docker inspect <image>` to see the layer digests and config. Example: `docker history ubuntu:latest` This shows each layer, the created-by command, and size (some entries are “missing” depending on how it was built, but it’s still useful).
Sometimes `docker history` shows `0B` layers. Why?

Those are usually metadata-only steps (like `CMD`, `ENV`, `WORKDIR`) or steps that didn’t change the filesystem in a way that affects layer size (rare). The key is that not every instruction adds bytes to the filesystem.
If images are read-only, how does a container write logs or temp files?

Through the container’s writable layer, and/or through mounted volumes. A container’s root filesystem is: - read-only image layers - plus a writable layer on top Writes go to the writable layer. For logs, many setups write to stdout/stderr and let Docker capture logs, rather than writing to files inside the container. But writing to files works too (it just ends up in the writable layer unless mounted).
Where do Docker logs live?

From the user perspective, you view them with: `docker logs <container>` Where they’re stored depends on the logging driver: - Default is often `json-file`, storing logs on the host under Docker’s data directory. - Other drivers can send logs to syslog, journald, fluentd, etc. You can configure logging per container.
If I run `docker run -it ubuntu bash`, then exit, the container stops. If I run it again, is it the same container?

If you run `docker run ...` again, it creates a new container. If you want to restart the same one, you’d: 1) Start it once with a name: `docker run -it --name myubuntu ubuntu bash` 2) Exit 3) Restart: `docker start -ai myubuntu` - `-a` attaches - `-i` keeps stdin open
What happens to the writable layer when the container stops?
It persists on disk until the container is removed. So changes you made remain if you restart that same container. But again, that’s not the same as changing the image.
So if I want to use a container as a “workspace,” I can just keep restarting it?

You can, and many people do for experiments. But be aware: - It can become messy and hard to reproduce. - It’s easy to accumulate state and forget what changed. A cleaner approach for a “workspace” is: - Use bind mounts to keep your working files on the host. - Use a container as a tool environment (compiler/interpreter) without storing important state inside it. But for learning, keeping a named container and restarting it is fine.
What does `--rm` do exactly?

`--rm` tells Docker to automatically remove the container when it exits. That means: - the container object is deleted, - its writable layer is deleted, - but any named volumes or bind mounts remain. It’s great for one-off commands: `docker run --rm -it python:3.12 python -c "print('hi')"`
But if I want to keep installed packages from `apt-get`, I shouldn’t use `--rm`, right?

If you’re relying on installing packages interactively and keeping them, then yes, `--rm` would delete the container and you’d lose that state. However, the better approach is to put those installs into a Dockerfile and build an image, then you can use `--rm` safely because the packages are in the image.
Okay, I think I’m getting it. But I still don’t fully see the “image is a template, container is an instance” thing. Can you give a more concrete example?

Sure. Imagine you build an image called `myweb:1.0` that contains your web server and code. Now you run: - `docker run -d --name web1 -p 8081:80 myweb:1.0` - `docker run -d --name web2 -p 8082:80 myweb:1.0` Same image, two containers. They: - share the same read-only image layers, - but each has its own writable layer (so if you edit `/etc/hostname` in one, it won’t affect the other), - each has its own network identity (different IPs on the Docker network), - each can be started/stopped independently. That’s “one template, multiple instances.”
If they share layers, does that mean security issues? Like can one container read another’s files?

Not via the filesystem layers. The shared layers are read-only and identical for all containers; they don’t contain container-specific secrets unless you baked secrets into the image (which you should avoid). Containers can’t directly read each other’s writable layers. Isolation is enforced by the kernel and Docker’s storage driver permissions. However, security depends on many factors: - If a container is privileged or has host mounts, it can break isolation. - If containers share a volume, they share data. - If a container escapes via a kernel exploit, all bets are off. But the “shared base layers” themselves don’t let containers read each other’s changes.
You mentioned “privileged.” What is that?

`--privileged` gives a container broad access to the host (all capabilities, device access, etc.). It’s often used for low-level tasks but is dangerous because it weakens isolation significantly. Safer alternatives: - Add only specific capabilities (`--cap-add`) - Mount only required devices - Avoid host mounts of sensitive directories For learning, you usually don’t need privileged containers.
I ran into `permission denied` when writing to a directory inside a container. Is that related to images being read-only?

Usually it’s related to Unix permissions and the user the process runs as, not the image being read-only. Even though the container has a writable layer, file permissions still apply. If you’re running as a non-root user inside the container, you may not be able to write to certain directories. You can check: - What user you are: `id` - Directory permissions: `ls -ld /path` You can run as root (not always recommended) with: `docker run -u 0 ...` or define `USER` in Dockerfile appropriately.
Can an image specify a default user?

Yes. In Dockerfile: `USER someuser` This sets the default user for running the container. If you don’t set it, many images default to root, though more security-conscious images set a non-root user. You can override at runtime with `docker run --user`.
How do environment variables fit into images vs containers?

Images can define default environment variables with `ENV` in the Dockerfile. Containers can set or override env vars at runtime with `-e` or `--env-file`. - Image ENV: baked-in defaults - Container env: runtime configuration, can differ per container Example: Image sets `ENV PORT=80` You run: `docker run -e PORT=8080 myimg` Inside container, `PORT` is 8080.
If I set an env var inside a running container (like `export FOO=bar`), does it persist?

Only for that shell/process and its child processes. It’s not persisted for the container as a whole. If you restart the container, that exported variable won’t be set unless it’s in the container config (`docker run -e`) or set in image metadata (`ENV`), or set by an entrypoint script.
Entry point scripts confuse me. What’s the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT?

In short: - `ENTRYPOINT` defines the main executable. - `CMD` defines default arguments to that executable (or the command if no ENTRYPOINT). Common patterns: 1) No ENTRYPOINT, CMD is the command: CMD ["python", "app.py"] Then `docker run image` runs that; `docker run image bash` overrides CMD entirely. 2) ENTRYPOINT as fixed executable, CMD as default args: ENTRYPOINT ["python"] CMD ["app.py"] Then `docker run image` runs `python app.py`. If you do `docker run image other.py`, it runs `python other.py` (CMD overridden, ENTRYPOINT stays). There’s also shell form vs exec form; exec form (JSON array) is generally preferred for signal handling.
Signal handling?

If you use shell form like: CMD python app.py Docker runs it via `/bin/sh -c ...`, which can interfere with signal propagation (like Ctrl+C / SIGTERM) and process PID 1 behavior. Exec form: CMD ["python","app.py"] runs Python directly as PID 1 in the container, which generally handles signals more predictably. PID 1 has special behavior in Linux (signal handling, zombie reaping), so people often use minimal init systems (like `tini`) in some cases.
PID 1 in a container is the main process?

Yes. The first process launched in the container’s PID namespace is PID 1. If that process exits, the container stops. Also, PID 1 doesn’t automatically reap zombie processes the same way typical init systems do, so if your application spawns child processes and doesn’t reap them, you can accumulate zombies. Some images use `tini` or `dumb-init` as ENTRYPOINT to handle that.
If I’m just running a simple app, do I need `tini`?

Often no, especially if your app doesn’t spawn unmanaged child processes. But for some stacks (like when using shell scripts that spawn background processes), it can help. Docker also has an option: `docker run --init ...` which runs a minimal init process as PID 1 to handle reaping.
Going back: you said images are immutable, but I can “tag” them. Is tagging changing the image?

Tagging doesn’t change the image content; it changes the reference name pointing to that image. An image is identified by its content digest. Tags are pointers. So `docker tag imageID myname:tag` just creates another tag pointing at the same underlying image. No layer changes.
If I build an image twice with the same Dockerfile, do I get the same image ID?

Not necessarily, even if the filesystem content is identical, because metadata like timestamps can differ. With BuildKit and reproducible build settings, you can get closer, but in general, image IDs can change across builds. However, caching means you might reuse layers and end up with the same underlying layer digests. If you want deterministic builds, you need to control sources, timestamps, and build steps carefully.
What’s the difference between image ID and digest?

Docker shows an “IMAGE ID” locally, which is a hash of the image config (not always the same as the registry digest). The digest you see when pulling (sha256:...) usually refers to the manifest digest in the registry. In practice: - Locally: `docker images` shows IMAGE ID (short). - Remotely/pull: you can pin with `@sha256:` (manifest digest). They’re related but not identical concepts.
I saw `REPOSITORY`, `TAG`, `IMAGE ID`, `CREATED`, `SIZE`. Size confuses me. Is that the total size?

The `SIZE` shown is the “virtual size” of the image (sum of its layers), but since layers can be shared between images, the actual disk usage might be less. For disk usage, `docker system df` gives a better view: - images total and reclaimable, - containers, - local volumes, - build cache.
If I have two images based on ubuntu, do they both count ubuntu’s size?

`docker images` will show each image’s size including shared layers, so it can look like you’re using more space than you are. On disk, shared layers are stored once. `docker system df` helps estimate actual usage and what can be reclaimed.
What about multi-stage builds? Are those images too?

Multi-stage builds let you use multiple `FROM` stages in one Dockerfile. Intermediate stages produce intermediate images/layers during the build, but typically only the final stage is tagged and kept (though build cache may keep intermediate layers for reuse). Example: - Stage 1 builds a binary with compilers. - Stage 2 copies just the binary into a small runtime image. This reduces final image size and attack surface.
Can you show a small example?

Example for a Go app: FROM golang:1.22 AS build WORKDIR /src COPY . . RUN go build -o app . FROM debian:stable-slim WORKDIR /app COPY --from=build /src/app /app/app CMD ["/app/app"] First stage has Go toolchain; second stage has only the compiled binary.
So the final image doesn’t include the build tools.
Exactly. The final image layers only include what you copy into it plus the base runtime layers.
Is there a way to see what files are inside an image without running it?

Not super directly with plain Docker CLI, but there are options: - Run a temporary container and inspect: `docker run --rm -it --entrypoint sh image` then `ls`, etc. - Use `docker export` on a created container (not running) and inspect the tar: `cid=$(docker create image)` `docker export $cid | tar -tv | head` `docker rm $cid` - Use third-party tools like `dive` to explore layers and file changes. For learning, the “run a shell” approach is simplest.
If I run a shell in an image, am I making a container?
Yes. Any `docker run` creates a container. Even if it’s just a shell for inspection, it’s still a container instance.
What’s the difference between `docker create` and `docker run`?

`docker create` creates a container from an image but does not start it. `docker run` = create + start (and optionally attach). So: - `docker create --name c1 ubuntu` creates the container object (stopped). - `docker start -ai c1` starts and attaches.
If I want to look at a container’s filesystem without starting it, can I?

Yes, to some extent: - You can `docker cp` files out of a stopped container. - You can `docker export` it. - You can inspect its mounts and metadata. Example: `docker cp mycontainer:/etc/os-release .` That works even if the container is stopped.
I tried `docker cp` and it worked, that’s neat. Does it work with images too?

`docker cp` works with containers, not images. But you can create a container from an image (without starting it) and then copy files out: `cid=$(docker create ubuntu)` `docker cp $cid:/etc/os-release ./os-release` `docker rm $cid` That’s a common trick.
Nice. Another confusion: when I pull an image, where does it go?

Docker stores images in its data directory, managed by the Docker daemon. The exact location depends on OS and configuration: - On Linux, often under `/var/lib/docker/` (e.g., overlay2 storage). - On Docker Desktop, it’s inside the VM. You normally don’t manipulate these directories directly; use Docker commands to manage them.
Can I have multiple versions of the same image?

Yes. You can have multiple tags (or digests) locally. For example: - `python:3.11` - `python:3.12` They may share layers but are distinct images. Also, you can tag the same image with multiple tags: `docker tag myimg:latest myimg:backup`
If tags are pointers, can two tags point to different images over time?

Yes. A tag can be updated to point to a new image. Example: `myimg:latest` today might point to image A, tomorrow to image B after rebuilding and pushing. Locally, if you rebuild `myimg:latest`, the tag will be moved to the newly built image. The old image might become dangling (untagged) unless another tag points to it.
That’s why people say don’t use `latest`?
That’s one reason. `latest` can change unexpectedly, so it’s not a reliable version. It’s okay for experiments, but for predictable setups, use explicit version tags or digests.
What about base images—should I always start with `ubuntu`?

Not necessarily. Base image choice depends on what you need: - `ubuntu` or `debian`: general-purpose, familiar package managers, but larger. - `alpine`: very small, uses musl libc; some binaries behave differently; can be trickier. - Distroless images: minimal runtime, no package manager/shell; great for production but harder to debug. - Language-specific slim images: `python:3.12-slim`, `node:20-slim`—often a good balance. For learning, `ubuntu` is fine because it’s familiar.
If I use `alpine`, does that change image vs container concept?

No, the concept is the same. It just changes the contents of the filesystem layers. But you might notice differences like: - `apk` instead of `apt` - `/bin/sh` is `ash` - smaller image size - some packages and glibc compatibility issues
I tried `alpine` and `bash` wasn’t there.
Right—Alpine typically doesn’t include bash by default; it has `sh`. You can install bash with: `apk add --no-cache bash` Or just use `sh`: `docker run -it alpine sh`
Okay. Another term: “registry.” Is that where images live?

Yes. A registry is a server that stores and serves container images (manifests and layers). Docker Hub is a public registry. Others include GitHub Container Registry, GitLab, and private registries. When you `docker pull name:tag`, Docker contacts the registry, fetches the manifest, downloads layers, verifies digests, and stores them locally.
When I do `docker pull ubuntu`, how does it know which registry?

If you don’t specify a registry domain, Docker defaults to Docker Hub (`docker.io`). So: - `ubuntu:latest` implies `docker.io/library/ubuntu:latest` The `library/` namespace is for “official images.” If you specify a domain like: `ghcr.io/someuser/someimage:tag` Docker pulls from that registry.
What are “official images”?

On Docker Hub, “official images” are curated images under the `library/` namespace (like `ubuntu`, `nginx`, `python`). They follow certain guidelines and are maintained with a higher level of scrutiny than random user images. Still, you should treat any image as third-party code and be mindful of trust, updates, and vulnerabilities.
How do I check if an image is safe?

There’s no perfect answer, but you can improve confidence by: - Using well-known official images or reputable publishers. - Pinning versions/digests. - Scanning images for vulnerabilities (tools like Trivy). - Keeping images updated. - Minimizing what you include (smaller attack surface). - Avoiding running as root when possible. But for learning on your own machine, the main thing is using trusted sources.
You mentioned vulnerability scanning. Is that part of Docker?

Docker Desktop includes some scanning features in certain editions, but scanning is often done with external tools. A popular one: - Trivy: `trivy image myimg:tag` It checks OS packages and language dependencies (depending on configuration).
If I update packages in a running container, does that improve security?

Only for that container instance, and only until it’s replaced. The more robust approach is: - Rebuild the image with updated base image and packages. - Redeploy containers from the updated image. Because in most workflows, containers get recreated. Updating inside a running container is a temporary patch at best.
How do base image updates work? If `ubuntu:latest` updates, do my images automatically get updates?

Not automatically. If your Dockerfile says `FROM ubuntu:latest`, then when you rebuild your image later, Docker will try to use the cached base image unless you pull a newer one. To ensure you get updates, you can: - `docker pull ubuntu:latest` before building - or build with `--pull`: `docker build --pull -t myimg .` Then rebuild your image so it includes the new base layers.
That makes sense. I’m still mixing up “image is static” and “container is dynamic.” Can you compare it to something in programming?

Think of an image like a class definition or a compiled binary artifact, and a container like an object instance or running process. - Image ~ compiled program + filesystem dependencies - Container ~ a running process from that program, with runtime state (memory, open files, temp data) Or: - Image ~ git commit snapshot - Container ~ a working directory with uncommitted changes The writable layer is like your working tree changes; the image layers are like commits.
That git analogy helps. So `docker commit` is like making a commit from the working tree?

Exactly. It captures the current container filesystem state into a new image layer stack, similar to committing changes. But like committing without a clear history message or reproducible steps, it can be messy compared to a Dockerfile.
If I want to learn, what commands should I practice to really internalize images vs containers?

A good mini-exercise: 1) Pull an image: `docker pull ubuntu:24.04` 2) Run a container, make a change: `docker run -it --name demo ubuntu:24.04 bash` Inside: `echo hello > /tmp/hello.txt` Exit. 3) Start it again and see the file is still there: `docker start -ai demo` `cat /tmp/hello.txt` 4) Create a new container from same image and see file is NOT there: `docker run --rm -it ubuntu:24.04 bash` `cat /tmp/hello.txt` (should fail) 5) Now commit the changed container to an image: `docker commit demo ubuntu-with-hello:1` Run a new container from the new image: `docker run --rm -it ubuntu-with-hello:1 bash` `cat /tmp/hello.txt` (should work) This demonstrates: - container writable layer persists across restarts - new containers from original image don’t see changes - committing turns container state into an image
That’s a great experiment. What about file changes with volumes—how would that differ?

Another exercise: 1) Create a named volume: `docker volume create v1` 2) Run a container mounting it: `docker run -it --name voltest -v v1:/data ubuntu:24.04 bash` Inside: `echo hi > /data/file.txt` Exit. 3) Remove the container: `docker rm voltest` 4) Run a new container with the same volume: `docker run --rm -it -v v1:/data ubuntu:24.04 bash` `cat /data/file.txt` (it’s still there) This shows volume persistence independent of containers and images.
What about bind mounts? Are they basically just sharing my folder?

Yes. Bind mounts map a host directory/file into the container. Example: `docker run --rm -it -v "$PWD":/work ubuntu bash` Then inside container, `/work` is your current host directory. Changes inside container affect your host files, and vice versa. It’s powerful for development, but be careful with permissions and not accidentally overwriting host files.
I once mounted my home directory and then ran commands as root in the container… and it created root-owned files on my host. That was annoying.

That’s a common gotcha. When you bind mount, file ownership/permissions are those of the host filesystem. If the container process runs as root and writes to the mount, the created files may be owned by root on the host. Ways to mitigate: - Run the container as your user ID: `docker run --user $(id -u):$(id -g) ...` - Adjust permissions carefully - Use named volumes for container-managed data rather than bind-mounting sensitive directories
Can you explain `--user` a bit more? How does it work inside the container?

`--user UID:GID` sets the numeric user and group IDs used by the container process. It doesn’t automatically create a username inside `/etc/passwd`, but Linux permissions are based on numeric IDs, so it works. Example: `docker run --rm -it --user 1000:1000 -v "$PWD":/work ubuntu bash` Inside, `id` will show UID 1000, but the username might not resolve. You can still read/write files according to host permissions. Some images include helper users; you can also create a user in the Dockerfile.
Does that affect images vs containers?
It’s runtime configuration (container-level). The image can set a default `USER`, but you can override it per container. Different containers from the same image can run as different users.
Okay. What about “container filesystem is ephemeral.” But we just said it persists when stopped.

“Ephemeral” in container discussions usually means: - not meant for long-term persistence - can be thrown away and recreated - not the right place for important data Technically, the writable layer persists until you remove the container. But operationally, containers are often removed/recreated, so anything stored only in the writable layer is at risk of disappearing. Thus: persistent state should be in volumes or external services.
I’ve heard that containers are “stateless.” But databases run in containers all the time. Isn’t that stateful?

The database process is stateful, but the container is treated as disposable. The persistent database files live in a volume (or external storage). So you can remove/recreate the DB container and keep the data. That’s the pattern: - Stateful app - Stateless container wrapper - Persistent volume
How do I list volumes?

`docker volume ls` Inspect a volume: `docker volume inspect v1` Remove a volume: `docker volume rm v1` Remove unused volumes: `docker volume prune` (careful; it deletes volumes not referenced by any container)
If I remove a container, does Docker know which volumes are unused?

It knows which volumes are referenced by existing containers. A volume can remain after containers are removed. If no containers reference it, it’s considered unused and could be pruned. But be careful: “unused” doesn’t mean “unimportant.” It just means no container currently references it.
Another confusion: I sometimes see the term “image layer cache” and “build cache.” Are they the same?

Related but not identical. - Image layers: the actual filesystem layer objects that make up images. - Build cache: metadata and intermediate results Docker/BuildKit uses to speed up builds, which may include layers and other artifacts. BuildKit can store additional cache beyond final image layers (e.g., cache mounts, intermediate stages). `docker builder prune` manages build cache.
What’s BuildKit?

BuildKit is the newer build engine for Docker. It improves: - parallel build steps - better caching - secrets and SSH forwarding (so you don’t bake secrets into images) - more efficient context handling - advanced features like cache mounts It’s often enabled by default in modern Docker.
Secrets and SSH forwarding?

Example: If you need to download private dependencies during build, you shouldn’t `COPY` your SSH key into the image. With BuildKit, you can do: - `RUN --mount=type=ssh git clone ...` or: - `RUN --mount=type=secret,id=mysecret ...` These mounts exist only during build steps and don’t end up in the final image layers. That’s more advanced, but it’s good to know the direction.
Still, I want to keep focused on basics. If I had to explain images vs containers to someone in one minute, what would I say?

“A Docker image is an immutable blueprint containing a filesystem snapshot and default settings. A Docker container is a runnable instance of that image: it adds a thin writable layer and runtime configuration, and it runs a process. You can create many containers from one image; changing a container doesn’t change the image.”
Good. Now, can we talk about what happens when I run `docker build`? Is it creating a container?

During `docker build`, Docker creates intermediate containers to execute `RUN` steps (conceptually). Each `RUN` step is executed in a temporary container created from the previous layer, then the result is committed as a new layer. With BuildKit, the internals differ a bit, but the high-level concept remains: build steps run in isolated environments and produce layers. So yes, builds involve containers (or container-like execution) under the hood.
That’s interesting—so containers are used to build images, and images are used to run containers.
Exactly. It’s a loop: - Build uses container execution to produce layers → image - Run uses image layers to create container instances → containers
If I change a file in my project and rebuild, how does Docker know which steps to rerun?

Through the cache keys. If you change a file that gets copied in a certain `COPY` step, that step’s cache is invalidated, and all subsequent steps are rebuilt. That’s why Dockerfile ordering matters for efficient rebuilds.
Let’s say I have a Node project. What’s a Dockerfile structure that helps caching?

Common pattern: FROM node:20-slim WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm ci COPY . . CMD ["npm","start"] This way: - Dependency install layer depends only on package.json / package-lock.json - App code changes don’t force reinstalling dependencies unless the manifests change Also use `.dockerignore` to exclude `node_modules` and other junk.
If I accidentally copy `node_modules` into the image, is that bad?

Often yes: - It bloats the image. - It can cause OS/architecture mismatches (host-built native modules may not work in the container). - It can break caching and reproducibility. Usually you want to install dependencies inside the image using `npm ci` and keep host `node_modules` out of the build context via `.dockerignore`.
Architecture mismatches?

If your host is macOS (especially Apple Silicon) and you copy `node_modules` built for that environment into a Linux container, native addons may not run. Even if your host is Linux, differences in libc or CPU can matter. Building dependencies inside the container ensures they match the container OS and architecture.
Speaking of architecture, I saw `linux/amd64` and `linux/arm64`. How do images handle that?

Many images are “multi-arch.” A tag like `ubuntu:24.04` can refer to a manifest list that contains different image variants for different architectures. Docker pulls the correct one for your system by default. You can specify platform: `docker pull --platform linux/amd64 ubuntu:24.04` This is useful if you’re on ARM but need amd64 (though emulation may be involved, like QEMU).
Does that mean `ubuntu:24.04` is not a single image?

At the registry level, the tag can refer to a manifest list (index) that points to multiple platform-specific images. Locally, after pulling, you have the specific image variant for your chosen platform. So the “tag” can represent a set of images.
If I build an image on my machine, is it tied to my architecture?
By default, yes. You build for your current platform. With Buildx (Docker’s extended builder), you can build multi-arch images, but that’s beyond the basics.
Okay. Another thing: I see “container ID” and “image ID.” Are those similar?

They’re both identifiers, but for different objects: - Image ID identifies an image (its config hash). - Container ID identifies a container instance (its metadata/config plus reference to image). Containers also have names (human-friendly). Images have repository:tag references.
If I remove a container, can I get it back?

Not really. Once removed, it’s gone (unless you had exported it or committed it). If you need to preserve data, use volumes or commit the container to an image before removing. You can sometimes recover data from Docker storage if you dig into `/var/lib/docker`, but that’s not a normal or reliable method.
So containers are meant to be disposable.
Yes—that’s the guiding philosophy.
What about “immutable images.” Can I modify an image in place?

Not in the sense of editing an existing image’s layers. Images are content-addressed; changing content would produce a new image with a new ID/digest. You can create a new image based on an existing one (build a new Dockerfile, or commit), but you don’t patch an image in place.
Yet I can tag an image differently. That’s kind of like modifying.
Tagging modifies your local metadata (and can modify registry references when you push), but it doesn’t modify the image content. Think of it like adding another label to the same immutable object.
Okay. What about pushing? When I `docker push`, what happens?

`docker push repo/name:tag` uploads the image’s layers and manifest to a registry. It: - checks which layers the registry already has (dedup), - uploads missing layers, - uploads the manifest that references those layers. If you push a new tag pointing to the same image, it might upload only a new manifest reference, not layers.
If layers are shared, is that why push can be fast sometimes?

Exactly. If the registry already has those layers (because you pushed before or because another image shares them), it can skip uploading them. Similarly, pulling can be faster if you already have some layers locally.
What is the difference between `docker pull` and just running `docker run`?
`docker pull` only downloads the image. `docker run` pulls the image if needed and then creates/starts a container. So `pull` is “get the template,” `run` is “instantiate and execute.”
Sometimes I want to just check if an image exists locally.

Use: - `docker image ls <name>` or `docker images <name>` - `docker image inspect <name>` (exits non-zero if not found) Example: `docker image inspect ubuntu:24.04 >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo yes || echo no`
Cool. Can you talk about `docker diff`? I saw it mentioned somewhere.

`docker diff <container>` shows filesystem changes made in a container relative to its image. It lists: - A = added files - C = changed files - D = deleted files This is a great command for seeing what changed in the writable layer. Example: `docker diff demo` It helps you understand the container vs image difference concretely.
That’s exactly the kind of tool I need. Does it work for stopped containers?
Yes, it works for stopped containers too (as long as the container still exists).
Nice. What about `docker commit` combined with `docker diff`?
