Great combination for learning: 1) Start container, make changes. 2) `docker diff` to see what changed. 3) `docker commit` to create a new image. 4) Run a new container from that new image and verify the changes are now part of the image. Just remember: for real projects, Dockerfiles are preferred.
I’m curious: when you commit a container, does it include volumes?

No. Volumes and bind mounts are external to the container filesystem. `docker commit` captures the container’s writable layer (and the underlying image layers) but not external mounted data. So if you wrote a file into `/data` where `/data` is a volume mount, committing won’t include that file.
That makes sense. Volumes are separate.
Exactly.
Another question: what’s the difference between `docker stop` and `docker kill`?

Both stop a running container, but: - `docker stop` sends SIGTERM, waits (default 10s), then SIGKILL if it doesn’t exit. - `docker kill` sends SIGKILL immediately (or a specified signal). `stop` is graceful; `kill` is forceful.
Does stopping a container remove it?
No. It just changes its state to “exited.” The container still exists until removed.
Is there a way to automatically restart containers?

Yes, restart policies: - `--restart=no` (default) - `--restart=on-failure[:max-retries]` - `--restart=always` - `--restart=unless-stopped` Example: `docker run -d --restart=unless-stopped nginx` Compose has `restart:` too.
Restart policy is container config, right?
Yes, it’s part of container runtime configuration, not image content.
If I create a container without a name, how do I refer to it later?

Docker assigns a random name (like `nervous_einstein`). You can see it in `docker ps -a`. You can refer by: - container ID (short ID works), - or that random name. You can also rename: `docker rename oldname newname`
I think naming containers helps.
It does, especially when you’re learning and want to restart or inspect the same container.
How does Docker decide the random names?
It uses a word list (adjective_scientist style). It’s just for convenience.
Another concept: what is `ENTRYPOINT` good for?

It’s good when your image is meant to behave like a single-purpose executable. For example, an image that always runs a specific server or CLI tool. It also helps when you want `docker run image args...` to pass args to the program, rather than replacing the program. Example: ENTRYPOINT ["curl"] CMD ["--help"] Then: - `docker run mycurl` shows help - `docker run mycurl https://example.com` runs curl to that URL
That’s neat. So the image can act like a packaged command.
Exactly.
If I run `docker run ubuntu echo hi`, does it override CMD?
Yes. It runs `echo hi` instead of the default command. If the image had an ENTRYPOINT, it might behave differently depending on how it’s set.
Okay. So image defines defaults, container can override.
Right. Container creation chooses the final command based on image ENTRYPOINT/CMD and runtime arguments.
I’m still curious about the “writable layer.” Where is it stored?

It’s stored in Docker’s storage backend (e.g., overlay2) under Docker’s data directory. Each container has its own “diff” directory representing changes. You typically don’t interact with it directly. Tools like `docker diff` and `docker inspect` are the supported ways.
If I have a huge file in a container writable layer, will it show up in image size?

No, it affects container size, not image size. You can see container disk usage with `docker ps -s` (shows size and virtual size), though it’s not always perfectly intuitive. For overall disk usage, `docker system df` helps.
So images take disk space, containers take disk space, volumes take disk space.

Exactly. Docker stores several categories: - Images (shared layers) - Containers (writable layers) - Volumes (persistent data) - Networks (small metadata) - Build cache Prune commands target different categories.
I’m worried about accidentally deleting something important with prune.

That’s a valid concern. Some safer habits: - Use `docker container prune` to remove stopped containers (usually safe if you don’t rely on them). - Avoid `docker volume prune` unless you’re sure. - Use `docker system df` first to see what’s using space. - Prefer removing specific objects by name when in doubt.
If I want to keep my Docker environment tidy while learning, what would you recommend?

A simple routine: - Use `--rm` for one-off runs. - Name containers you intend to reuse. - Periodically run `docker ps -a` and remove old stopped containers you don’t need. - Avoid leaving lots of anonymous volumes around; prefer named volumes when you want persistence. - Use `.dockerignore` to avoid bloated contexts.
Sounds good. Another thing: What is “scratch” base image?

`scratch` is an empty base image—literally nothing. You use it for minimal images, usually for statically compiled binaries (like Go) or for cases where you copy in everything you need. Example: FROM scratch COPY mybinary /mybinary CMD ["/mybinary"] No shell, no package manager, nothing else.
So then the container only has my binary and whatever I copy.
Exactly.
But then debugging is hard.
Yes. Minimal images reduce attack surface and size but are harder to debug. A common approach is to have: - a debug image (with shell/tools) - a production image (distroless/scratch)
Okay. Returning to my original confusion: Is it fair to say images are like “files” and containers are like “processes”?

That’s a good approximation: - Image = artifact stored on disk - Container = running instance (process) using that artifact Just remember: containers include filesystem state (writable layer) and config, so they’re a bit more than “just a process,” but process is the core.
If I stop a container, it’s no longer a process. But it still exists. So it’s like a saved process state?

Not the process memory state—Docker doesn’t preserve RAM state when stopping normally. It preserves the container object: - filesystem writable layer - configuration - logs (depending) But not the in-memory state of running programs. So it’s more like a stopped VM without saved RAM—disk persists, memory doesn’t.
That distinction helps. So containers don’t pause and resume like that?

Docker has `docker pause`/`unpause` which uses cgroup freezer to suspend processes (they stay in memory). But if you `stop`, processes exit and memory state is lost. Pause is more like “freeze,” stop is “terminate gracefully.”
If I pause a container and then unpause, is it the same process?
Yes, it resumes the same processes. But pause is usually for short-term; if the host reboots, it won’t preserve.
Great. I think I have it now. But I want to be sure: can you give me a few “trick questions” about images vs containers to test myself?

Sure—answer these: 1) If you run `docker run --rm ubuntu:24.04 bash` and create a file in `/tmp`, then exit—will the file exist if you run the same command again? 2) If you build an image and then edit a file inside a running container created from it—does the image change? 3) If you mount a named volume at `/data` and write a file there, then remove the container—does the file remain? 4) Can two running containers from the same image have different environment variables? 5) If you remove an image, does it automatically remove all containers created from it?

Okay, my answers: 1) No, because `--rm` deletes the container. 2) No, only the container changes. 3) Yes, volume persists. 4) Yes, because env vars are set per container. 5) No, I think you have to remove containers first.
All correct. For #5, Docker also usually won’t let you remove an image that’s referenced by existing containers unless you force it, but it won’t automatically remove those containers.
Nice. One last thing: If I want to see a container’s config and how it differs from the image’s defaults, how do I do that?

Use `docker inspect` for both and compare. - Container config: `docker inspect <container>` Look at fields like: - `.Config.Env` - `.Config.Cmd` - `.Config.Entrypoint` - `.HostConfig.PortBindings` - `.Mounts` - Image config: `docker inspect <image>` Look at: - `.Config.Env` - `.Config.Cmd` - `.Config.Entrypoint` - `.Config.ExposedPorts` The container’s config is derived from the image defaults plus runtime overrides. Inspecting both helps you see what came from where.
That’s really helpful. I think I can move forward now without mixing them up.

Good. If you want, tell me what you’re trying to containerize (language/framework) and I can suggest a Dockerfile that makes the image/container boundary clear, plus a couple commands to experiment with layers and volumes.

I need a regex that matches dates in DD/MM/YYYY format but not DD-MM-YYYY. What am I doing wrong with this pattern: `\b\d{2}[-/]\d{2}[-/]\d{4}\b`? It keeps matching the dash version too, and sometimes it matches inside longer strings.

Your pattern explicitly allows either `-` or `/` in both separator positions because of `[-/]`. If you want to match only `DD/MM/YYYY`, use `/` literally in both places: `\b\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}\b`. If you also want to prevent matching inside longer digit sequences, `\b` is usually okay but can behave unexpectedly around underscores and non-ASCII word chars. A safer boundary for “not preceded or followed by a digit” is: - `(?<!\d)\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}(?!\d)` That will match `12/03/2024` but not `12-03-2024`, and not `112/03/2024` where it’s embedded in digits.

Also, I need to extract the date from lines like `DATE=12/03/2024;` and `DATE: 12/03/2024`, but not from `DATE=12-03-2024;`. And I keep getting partial matches like it matches `12/03/202` when the year is 2024 but I’m running it in some “find” box.

A few things are likely happening: 1) Some “find” boxes don’t support `\b` as a word boundary, or they treat it differently (sometimes as backspace). In those environments, `\b` won’t do what you expect. 2) Partial matches like `12/03/202` usually happen when: - the engine is not actually using a regex (or using a different syntax), or - there’s a quantifier/backtracking issue due to surrounding pattern, or - your tool is doing “incremental” or “lazy” matching display. To be robust and also capture from `DATE=...` and `DATE: ...`, you can anchor to the prefix: - `\bDATE\s*[:=]\s*(\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4})\b` But again, if `\b` is unreliable, prefer digit lookarounds: - `\bDATE\s*[:=]\s*(?<!\d)(\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4})(?!\d)` Or without `\b` at all: - `DATE\s*[:=]\s*(?<!\d)(\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4})(?!\d)` If your tool doesn’t support lookbehind (`(?<!\d)`), you can do a “manual boundary”: - `(?:^|[^\d])(\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4})(?!\d)` But note that this includes the leading non-digit in the overall match unless you use a capturing group for the date and read group 1.

Ok, but I’m in JavaScript sometimes (Node) and sometimes in Python. And also in VS Code search. In JS lookbehind is not always supported depending on runtime, right? I want one regex that I can basically reuse. Also, I need to validate real dates, not 39/99/0000. But I don’t want an insane regex either. Still, at least block impossible days/months.

Right: lookbehind support in JavaScript depends on engine/version; modern Node supports it, but older environments and some tools may not. VS Code uses JavaScript regex (ECMAScript), and it supports lookbehind in current versions, but not all older builds. If you want maximum portability across JS + Python + “search boxes”, avoid lookbehind. Use explicit boundaries and capture groups. For a reusable pattern that matches `DD/MM/YYYY` with basic day/month ranges (not perfect calendar validation), you can use: - `(?:^|[^\d])((?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/\d{4})(?!\d)` Explanation: - `(?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])` = 01–31 - `(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])` = 01–12 - `\d{4}` = 4-digit year - `(?:^|[^\d])` = start or a non-digit before (portable “left boundary”) - `(?!\d)` = not followed by digit (right boundary) - The date itself is in capture group 1. If you need to allow it after `DATE=` or `DATE:` specifically, you can incorporate that: - `DATE\s*[:=]\s*((?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/\d{4})` This won’t match `DATE=12-03-2024` because it requires `/`. If you truly need “real date” validation (e.g., reject 31/04/2024, reject 29/02/2023), regex alone becomes complex. A practical approach: regex to parse and range-check, then validate with code (Date object / datetime).

I get that, but I’m still getting matches inside things like `XDATE=12/03/2024Y` where I don’t want it unless `DATE` is a standalone key. Yes: use “not a word char” boundaries that are more explicit than `\b`. A portable approach: - `(?:^|[^A-Za-z0-9_])DATE(?:$|[^A-Za-z0-9_])` But that consumes the surrounding delimiter characters. If you want to match `DATE=...` specifically and ensure DATE is a standalone token before `:` or `=`, you can require a non-word boundary on the left and then require `:` or `=` immediately after optional spaces: - `(?:^|[^A-Za-z0-9_])DATE\s*[:=]\s*((?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/\d{4})(?!\d)` This prevents matching inside `UPDATE=...` because before `DATE` there is `P` (a word character), so the `(?:^|[^A-Za-z0-9_])` won’t match there. If you need the overall match to start at `DATE` (not include the delimiter), you can capture DATE separately or use a capturing group for the left boundary and ignore it, but many tools only return the whole match. In code, you’d use group 1 for the date anyway. In Python, you can also use `(?<![A-Za-z0-9_])DATE(?![A-Za-z0-9_])`, but that’s lookbehind again. The consuming-boundary version is more portable.

I actually don’t want that; I want only lines that start with `DATE` as the key, not `SOME-DATE`. So it should be start-of-line only. But there may be whitespace before it.

Then anchor to the start of the line (multiline mode in code; in VS Code search, `^` and `$` work per line). Try: - `^\s*DATE\s*[:=]\s*((?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/\d{4})(?!\d)` This enforces: - start of line `^` - optional whitespace `\s*` - literal key `DATE` - `:` or `=` - the date with `/` This will not match `SOME-DATE=...` because `DATE` is not at the beginning (after optional whitespace). If your data may have leading indentation or tabs, `^\s*` covers it.

If you want to allow comments after, like `# ...` or `// ...`, you can extend the tail: - `^\s*([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)\s*[:=]\s*((?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/\d{4})\s*(?:;)?\s*(?:#.*|//.*)?$`

For multiple key-value pairs on one line, don’t anchor to line start/end. Instead, match “token boundaries” around the key and allow separators like `;` or start-of-string. A portable “global match” pattern: - `(?:^|[;\s])([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)\s*[:=]\s*((?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/\d{4})(?=$|[;\s])` Explanation: - `(?:^|[;\s])` ensures the key starts after start, semicolon, or whitespace (adjust if commas are used) - capture key and date - `(?=$|[;\s])` ensures the date ends before end, semicolon, or whitespace This avoids matching inside `SOMEKEYCREATED=...` because it requires a delimiter before the key. If you also allow commas as separators, use `[;\s,]` in both places.

Regex engines won’t normally “stop early” on a fixed `{4}` quantifier unless something else in the pattern forces it (like alternation or a following constraint that causes backtracking to a shorter match). Common causes: 1) You actually have `\d{3,4}` or `\d{1,4}` somewhere (maybe copied version). 2) There’s an alternation where one branch allows fewer digits. 3) The tool is not in regex mode, and `{4}` is being treated literally or differently. 4) The tool uses a different escape syntax (some require double escaping). 5) Your input contains invisible characters (e.g., `01/02/202\u200b4`) so it looks like 2024 but has a zero-width char splitting it; then `\d{4}` won’t match because digits aren’t contiguous, and it might match a different branch if present. If you can share the exact pattern and the exact tool/environment, we can narrow it down. If you can’t, a defensive technique is to capture the year explicitly and require a delimiter after it (end, semicolon, whitespace). That makes “partial year” less likely because it won’t satisfy the tail: - `(\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4})(?=$|[;\s])` If the tool is not actually doing regex, then nothing will behave consistently.

Notepad++ uses Boost/PCRE depending on version/settings; ripgrep uses Rust regex (which does NOT support lookaround at all), and JS supports lookaround in newer versions but not all. So the “lowest common denominator” is basically: no lookaround, no backreferences for validation logic, limited features. If you need one pattern that works in ripgrep too, avoid lookahead/lookbehind. That means you can’t do `(?=$|[;\s])` either. You can still do a lot, but you’ll have to accept either: - matching with some extra delimiter included, or - matching a broader region and post-filter in code. For ripgrep-compatible pattern to find `KEY=DD/MM/YYYY` and avoid `DD-MM-YYYY`, do: - `([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)[[:space:]]*[:=][[:space:]]*((0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/[0-9]{4})` That should work in Rust regex (ripgrep). It will match even if followed by digits, though; Rust regex can’t do lookahead to assert what comes next. You can approximate by including an allowed trailing delimiter in the match: - `([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)[[:space:]]*[:=][[:space:]]*((0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/[0-9]{4})([^0-9]|$)` Now you’re requiring a non-digit or end after the year, but you’re consuming that char (group 4). This is portable and works in ripgrep. In JS/Python you can use similar. In JS, POSIX classes like `[[:space:]]` aren’t supported; use `\s` there. So truly one pattern across all three is tough because of character class syntax differences too. If you must unify, choose a conservative set: `[ \t]` for spaces/tabs, `[0-9]` for digits. Example: - `([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)[ \t]*[:=][ \t]*((0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/[0-9]{4})([^0-9]|$)` That’s likely to work in ripgrep, Notepad++, JS, and Python.

That’s helpful. But I need to actually extract the date without the trailing delimiter char you added. In ripgrep, I can’t really “return group 2” easily unless I do replacement. In JS/Python I can, but in search tools it’s annoying.

In pure search tools, you often can’t “extract only capture group” without replacement. Two options: 1) Accept that the match includes one extra char and visually ignore it. 2) Use a replacement feature to capture only the group you want (Notepad++ supports replace with `$2` etc; ripgrep can output only matches with `-o` but it outputs the whole match, not a group). If you want the whole match to be exactly the date (no trailing delimiter) while still preventing digit-following, you need lookahead `(?!\d)` or `(?=$|[^\d])`, which ripgrep doesn’t support. So there’s a tradeoff: portability vs exact-match extraction. If your main purpose is finding/validating in files, consider matching the whole key-value pair instead, then you can extract the date later. Example: - `([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)[ \t]*[:=][ \t]*((0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/[0-9]{4})` This will still match in `...2024123`, but it will at least highlight the suspicious line. If you truly need to reject those, you need either lookaround or a delimiter-consuming trick.

The standard workaround is exactly what you did: include the “next char” in the match and ensure it’s not a digit (or it’s end). You can then use a capture group for the date and potentially use a tool that can print capture groups (ripgrep itself can’t, but you can pipe to something else). Example pipeline approach: - Use ripgrep to find candidate lines, then parse with a second tool (awk/python) to extract/validate groups. Or, if you’re okay matching full lines that contain only `KEY=DATE` and optional delimiter, you can anchor and avoid lookahead by consuming end-of-line: - `^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*[ \t]*[:=][ \t]*(0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/[0-9]{4}[ \t]*;?[ \t]*$` This doesn’t need lookahead because `$` is a real anchor supported by ripgrep. But it only works if the date is at end-of-line (aside from spaces/semicolon).

If the delimiter after the date is a space, semicolon, or end, you can bake that in without lookahead by consuming the delimiter character (space/semicolon/end). You can still capture the date separately. Example for `CREATED=...` followed by either whitespace, semicolon, or end: - `CREATED[ \t]*=[ \t]*((0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/[0-9]{4})([ \t;]|$)` Then you can continue matching other fields after because you’ve consumed one delimiter char (space or `;`). For multiple fields, you’d run a global find for each key-value pair rather than one giant regex, or you build a more complex repeated group. If you want to parse multiple `KEY=VALUE` tokens in a single pass, you can do tokenization: - Match `([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)=([^ \t;]+)` globally, then for specific keys validate the value with a second regex. That’s often simpler and more robust than one regex that validates everything.

Then you’re effectively describing a lexer: tokens separated by spaces/semicolons, values either unquoted (no spaces/semicolons) or quoted (allow escaped quotes). Regex can do it, but it becomes more than a date regex. A reasonable compromise: 1) Use a regex to match `KEY=VALUE` tokens where VALUE is either a quoted string or an unquoted atom. 2) For keys that should be dates, validate the VALUE with a date regex. Token regex (works in many engines; no lookbehind): - `([A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*)[ \t]*=[ \t]*(?:"([^"\\]*(?:\\.[^"\\]*)*)"|([^ \t;]+))` Groups: 1) key 2) quoted value (without outer quotes), allowing escapes 3) unquoted value Then when key is `CREATED`/`MODIFIED`, validate the value (from group 2 or 3) with: - `^(0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/[0-9]{4}$` In JS/Python this is straightforward. In search tools, it’s harder because you don’t have conditional logic. But for actual parsing/validation, code is the right layer.

You can approximate “not in a URL path” by asserting that it’s not preceded by `://` or by a slash. But this gets heuristic. Common simple heuristic: require that the character before the date is not `/` and not `:` (to avoid `://` and path segments). For JS/Python: - `(?<![\d/:])(?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(?:19\d{2}|20\d{2})(?![\d/])` This prevents matches where immediately before the day is `/` or `:` or a digit. In a URL path, the day segment is typically preceded by `/`, so it won’t match. But it will also prevent matching dates that are written as `.../12/03/2024` in other contexts (like file paths). If that’s acceptable, good. If you only want to exclude `http://` and `https://`, you can do a more targeted negative lookbehind for `://` (variable length lookbehind is not allowed in JS; Python allows fixed-length only; `://` is fixed length 3, so ok): - `(?<!://)(?<!\d)(?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(?:19\d{2}|20\d{2})(?![\d/])` But this still matches `/12/03/2024` in URLs because the immediate preceding chars are `m/` not `://`. So you’d also need `(?<!/)`: - `(?<!/)(?<!\d)(?:...)(?![\d/])` Again, heuristic.

If it matched dash ones, one of these is true: 1) The tool isn’t using regex mode (so `/` is treated as “any separator” or the UI is doing something else). 2) You edited one place but not both (e.g., `\d{2}[-/]\d{2}/\d{4}` still allows dash in first separator). 3) The tool has “extended” syntax where `/` is not literal (unlikely). 4) You’re looking at previous search highlights that didn’t refresh, or the tool caches matches. 5) The text contains a Unicode slash-like character or hyphen-like character and the tool normalizes display. In normal regex, `/` is just a literal slash in most flavors (JS, Python, PCRE). It will never match `-`. So if that happens, it’s almost certainly a tool/configuration issue.

Python (named groups): - `(?<!\d)(?P<day>0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?P<month>0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(?P<year>19\d{2}|20\d{2})(?![\d/])` JavaScript (named capture groups, requires modern JS): - `(?<!\d)(?<day>0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])/(?<month>0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(?<year>19\d{2}|20\d{2})(?![\d/])` Then replacement: - Python: `re.sub(pattern, r'\g<year>-\g<month>-\g<day>', text)` - JS: `text.replace(regex, '$<year>-$<month>-$<day>')`

Yes, use a left boundary that’s either start or a non-digit, and capture the date parts; the boundary will be in group 1: - `(^|[^\d])(?<day>0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])\/(?<month>0[1-9]|1[0-2])\/(?<year>19\d{2}|20\d{2})(?![\d/])` When replacing, you need to keep the boundary (group 1) so you don’t delete a character: - `text.replace(regex, '$1$<year>-$<month>-$<day>')` Note: In JS replacement, `$1` refers to the first capturing group (the boundary). Named groups start after that.

These patterns are relatively safe: they’re fixed-length alternatives, no nested quantifiers that cause catastrophic backtracking. The engine will try a few branches for day/month, but it’s constant-time per position. For performance: - Prefer `[0-9]` over `\d` if you want ASCII-only and predictable behavior. - Avoid leading `.*` before the date. - Use global matching with a straightforward regex and iterate. Example ASCII-only, efficient: `(?<![0-9])(?:0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(?:19[0-9]{2}|20[0-9]{2})(?![0-9/])`
Alright. Summarize the final recommended regexes: (1) exact match JS/Python with lookarounds, (2) JS without lookbehind, (3) ripgrep-compatible.

(1) JS/Python exact (lookbehind + lookahead), years 1900–2099, reject trailing digit or slash: `(?<![0-9])(?:0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(?:0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(?:19[0-9]{2}|20[0-9]{2})(?![0-9/])` (2) JS no lookbehind (captures left boundary as group 1; date parts named; reject trailing digit or slash): `(^|[^0-9])(?<day>0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(?<month>0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(?<year>19[0-9]{2}|20[0-9]{2})(?![0-9/])` (3) ripgrep-compatible (no lookaround; consumes boundaries; date is group 2): `(^|[^0-9])((0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])/(0[1-9]|1[0-2])/(19[0-9]{2}|20[0-9]{2}))([^0-9/]|$)` If you need the match to be only the date in ripgrep output, that’s not possible without lookaround; you’d use capture groups in a secondary tool or accept the boundary chars in the match.
